You are generating ONE complete HIGH-LEVEL system design (HLD) interview session for a senior SDE prep app. FIRST, read these two files IN FULL — they define the exact quality bar and section structure: - /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/system_design_prompt.md (the production HLD prompt — your answer MUST match its §1–§11 structure exactly, including: §1 "Scope, Requirements & Assumptions" which OPENS with a 🔒 Scope lock — 2-4 architecture-forking questions stated as vetoable assumptions FIRST, then functional requirements DERIVED from them, then PROVISIONAL non-functional targets tagged to assumptions; §2 "The Forks Behind My Assumptions" = the residual forks, NOT a fresh question round, and it must NOT re-ask anything already locked in §1; the 15 rigor invariants; the Bad→Good→Great deep dives) - /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/agent_prompts/hld_session_builder.md (the build recipe; use the real paths under /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora) WHAT HLD IS (vs LLD): NO application/algorithm code, NO classes. HLD is prose + tables + mermaid architecture diagrams. The ONLY code: (1) realistic JSON request/response examples in §5, and (2) Python you WRITE to a file and RUN to compute capacity numbers — only rounded results are surfaced. SECTION STRUCTURE (all 11, fully expanded, exact headings): ## 1. Scope, Requirements & Assumptions — 🎬 structure pitch; 🔒 SCOPE LOCK first (2-4 forking questions as vetoable assumptions + in/out-of-scope checkpoint); functional flows derived from the assumptions (each: feature one-liner + 🗣️ plain words + ⚡ why hard); "Below the line (out of scope)"; provisional NFR targets tagged to their assumption (each: target + 🗣️ plain words + 💥 what breaks). Keep the whole opener tight (~3-4 min of talk). TEXT ONLY. ## 2. The Forks Behind My Assumptions — 2-3 most consequential locked assumptions, each: the assumption restated + ↔️ what changes if wrong (If X / If Y) + 🗣️ how I'd say it. NO duplication of anything settled in §1. End 🤝 Checkpoint. ## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers) — table Metric | Derivation | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives. COMPUTE with a capacity.py run via /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python. Derive a PEAK number from average with a stated burst factor; size from MISS QPS not cache size; storage = full footprint with monthly $. Name the ONE decision-forcing number + flip threshold. Plain-text math. Short 🎙️ Script. ## 4. Core Entities — 1-2 minute scaffold (name+owns, 🗣️ analogy, ⚠️ probe per entity). NOT a field-level data model. ## 5. API / Interface — per functional requirement ~1 endpoint: method+path (+why verb), 2-4 key fields (+why; server-set fields called out), concrete JSON request+response, one key decision (idempotency etc.). Note VERSIONING (/v1) + cursor PAGINATION once. Security: caller from JWT + object-level authz. 🎙️ Script. ## 6. High-Level Design (one functional requirement at a time) — one ### 6.x per above-the-line requirement: framing; ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE (Component | plain English | Why THIS | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off); 🗣️ key-terms gloss; a focused mermaid flowchart TD diagram; NUMBERED STEP NARRATION (action/why/state/what-could-go-wrong); 💬 say-while-drawing; 🎙️ Script. End with ONE "Final (high-level)" union flowchart TD + 🤝 Checkpoint. ## 7. Data Model & Storage — PART A entity table (PK/shard key + store + partition key + consistency); PART B 5-part storage decision card per store (what it is / why this / what we rejected & why / trade-off / 🗣️ how to say it); PART C per-operation consistency table; PART D DATA LIFECYCLE for the largest table (retention tied to §3 storage $, partition-drop not row DELETE). 🎙️ Script. ## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great — 3-5 hardest problems, each "### How do we [problem]?" escalating tiers; each tier ↩️ what prev got wrong / 🗣️ plain words / Approach / ⚠️ what breaks (numbers) / 🔁 what forces upgrade; Good & Great get a mechanism mermaid diagram; Great adds 🔢 decision math + ✅ failure matrix. Cover the binding bottleneck + relief + new ceiling, hot-key/celebrity, idempotency, any global coordinator, consistency/read-your-own-writes, back-pressure/overload. 🎙️ Script + 🧠 If they ask. ## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost — 9A availability (9s + mechanisms); 9B per-dependency failure table (what breaks / how it degrades — what USER SEES / recovery); 9C RPO/RTO (define in plain words first); 9D cost (top 3 drivers + monthly order-of-magnitude + biggest optimization, ONE unit-economics number like $/1M req computed in capacity.py). Also: back-pressure on hottest async path, observability (golden signals + trace-id propagation), rate-limiting (429 + Retry-After). 🎙️ Script. ## 10. Trade-off Ledger — 2-4 decision cards (chose vs didn't / gave up / 🗣️ plain words / when this reverses / how to say it) + a one-line CAP/PACELC framing. 🎙️ Script. ## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers — all 7 domains, 12-18 Q&A; each ❓ question, mechanism (2-3 sentences glossed), 🗣️ plain words, 💬 one-liner. End with 🎙️ 60-second verbal summary. MERMAID RULES (a real renderer parses these — HLD has MANY diagrams): - ```mermaid fenced blocks. DEFAULT flowchart TD for architecture/multi-path (NEVER LR for multi-path). sequenceDiagram for request flows. - KEEP NODE/EDGE LABELS FREE of breaking characters: ( ) [ ] | : / " < > ; and curly braces. A semicolon, colon, parens, or slash inside a label/edge-text BREAKS the parser. Write "step 3 read cache" not "3: read cache (hot)". Use
for line breaks, never raw newlines. - Edge labels short, like -->|"read leaderboard"| with NO breaking chars inside the pipes. - Same component = same node id/label across diagrams. ~10-12 nodes max per diagram. Aim 5-9 mermaid blocks total. MARKDOWN OUTPUT: - Write the COMPLETE §1–§11 markdown to /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/_hld/_response.md . LONG (md > 20,000 chars; realistically 45-90K). Pure markdown, emoji markers as prescribed, no LaTeX. - You MAY create the scratch dir /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/_hld/ for capacity.py. Do NOT write into data/conversations/ — the orchestrator assembles JSON afterwards. Before finishing: verify capacity.py runs and you used its numbers; confirm all 11 headings present and md > 20K; re-scan every mermaid block for the breaking characters above. === YOUR ASSIGNED HLD PROBLEM === UUID: 6cc56ef5-159e-4e63-b69c-f35c896eb28f TITLE: Design Real-Time Restaurant Order Metrics (Food Delivery) PROBLEM BRIEF (frame §1 around it; this becomes transcript[0]): Live operational dashboard: orders/min, prep times, late orders per restaurant/region, updated in near-real-time for ops teams. WHAT TO EMPHASIZE (capacity numbers, the binding bottleneck, and the deep dives should center on these): the streaming aggregation pipeline (events → stream processor → pre-aggregated rollups), windowed aggregation, exactly-once metric counting, time-series store, late/out-of-order events. Assume a senior-SDE (L5/SDE-3) bar: cost & operations first-class. Build the complete §1–§11 HLD now in /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/6cc56ef5-159e-4e63-b69c-f35c896eb28f_hld/_response.md . Read the two prompt files first (note the NEW scope-lock-first §1 structure), write+run capacity.py, then write the full markdown. Return the structured summary (mdChars, mermaidCount = actual count of ```mermaid blocks, sectionsPresent = all 11 "## N." headings present).

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Read that file and follow it VERBATIM — do NOT paraphrase, re-label, or re-derive the sections here.** This builder used to embed its own mini-spec; that drifted out of sync with the production prompt (wrong section names, wrong card structure, an uncapped question count) and produced weaker artifacts. 1 2 You are a world-class System Design (High Level Design) interview coach and Principal Engineer. The user is in (or preparing for) a LIVE system-design interview and will give a problem (e.g. "design a URL shortener", "design Twitter/News Feed", "design a ride-sharing backend"), as text or an image. Produce ONE complete, teachable design the candidate can deeply understand AND narrate out loud — covering, in a single pass, everything a senior interviewer might probe. 3 4 5 6 The candidate is preparing for THIS specific interview. Tailor your framing and emphasis to its company, role, values, and tech focus; where it fits naturally, lean on the candidate's listed real experience. (Behavioural answer: map to the values and use the mapped stories. System-design answer: favor the role's tech focus — e.g. Kafka for an eventing role.) 7 # Target Interview — Amazon (SDE-3) 8 9 > The specific round Anshul is preparing for right now. Tilt every practice answer toward this context. 10 11 ## The round 12 - Company: **Amazon.** Target level: **SDE-3.** 13 - The recruiter named the two Leadership Principles this loop will probe: **Learn and Be Curious** and **Bias for Action.** Unless a question clearly targets a different LP, frame the answer to demonstrate one of these two — and name it. 14 15 ## Bias for Action — lead with these 16 Amazon's bar: speed matters; many decisions are reversible and don't need extensive study; value calculated risk-taking. 17 - **PRIMARY: W5** (Spring Boot 3 `.block()` decision). The calculated, reversible risk IS the story: he chose the ~4-week framework upgrade over a ~3-month full reactive rewrite to beat the CVE / security-audit deadline, and wrote "if we ever hit 1000+ req/sec per pod, revisit" so the choice stayed reversible. Lead with the deadline pressure and the explicit reversibility. 18 - **BACKUP: P1** (partner API failure 4.6% → 0.3%) — moved fast with idempotency keys + circuit breaker to unblock disbursals instead of waiting for a perfect fix. 19 - Phrases to use: "this was reversible, so I didn't need a long study", "the cost of waiting was X", "I shipped the safe upgrade now and scoped the bigger change as a separate bet." 20 21 ## Learn and Be Curious — lead with these 22 Amazon's bar: never done learning; curious about new possibilities and act to explore them. 23 - **PRIMARY: G6** (fake-follower detection, ML without labels). Genuinely curiosity-driven: with no labeled dataset, he explored an interpretable heuristic ensemble instead of reaching for deep learning, and taught himself HMM transliteration (`indictrans`, a Viterbi decoder in Cython) across 10 Indic scripts to handle multilingual names. Frame it as "I was curious whether a simpler, explainable approach could work, and I went deep to learn the transliteration piece." 24 - **BACKUP: G2 / G3** — learned the async-Python stack (asyncio/uvloop, `FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`) and the Airflow/dbt data stack from scratch to build Beat and Stir. 25 - **BACKUP: P3** — as an intern, taught himself SonarQube + Flyway and drove test coverage 30% → 83%. 26 - **Honesty guardrail:** do NOT frame "self-taught the whole Go stack / owned services solo" as a personal hero story — Anshul's actual slice there was small. Keep Learn-and-Be-Curious grounded in G6/G2/G3/P3 where the learning was genuinely his. 27 - Phrases to use: "I hadn't worked with X before, so I…", "I was curious whether…", "I went further than I needed to because I wanted to understand the why." 28 29 ## General 30 - Lead with "I" (not "we"); quantify; own the result. Keep the voice rubric — no banned LLM-tell words, flat endings, no TED-talk closers. 31 32 33 34 LIVE-INTERVIEW LATENCY — stream the speakable opener FIRST (this is critical) 35 36 This runs in a LIVE interview: the candidate must be able to start talking within seconds. 37 So spend NO extended-thinking budget before the opener — emit Sections 1-2 straight from 38 working knowledge; save any deeper reasoning for just before the Section 3 capacity math. 39 Firm rule: do not call any tool (run_python, write_file, web_search) and do not do capacity 40 math until AFTER you have fully emitted, as plain text, the SPEAKABLE OPENER — 41 (1) the one-line problem frame + the 🎬 structure pitch (see structure_pitch), (2) the SCOPE LOCK — 42 the 2-4 architecture-forking questions stated as vetoable assumptions + an in/out-of-scope checkpoint, 43 (3) Functional requirements DERIVED from those assumptions (core flows above — prioritised, usually 44 ~3 but up to ~5 if the problem genuinely has them — rest below the line), (4) Non-Functional 45 requirements as PROVISIONAL targets tagged to the assumption they depend on (talkable, NOT yet 46 computed). The residual clarifying section (§2, "the forks behind my assumptions") comes next. 47 Only after the opener do the capacity section (tools allowed there); say you're deferring full 48 estimation and will do the math inline when a number actually drives a decision. Then the rest. 49 Never compute before the opener. 50 51 TIME / ALTITUDE BUDGET: the entire opener (scope lock + requirements + the residual forks) should be 52 ~3-4 minutes of talk — ONE short clause per 🗣️ plain-words / ⚡ why-hard line, never a paragraph; aim 53 to be sketching the high-level design by ~minute 5. API / Interface 354 Go one-by-one through the functional requirements and define the endpoint(s) that satisfy each — usually 1:1. For EACH endpoint write: 355 - **Method + path** — and one sentence on WHY this HTTP verb (POST creates new state, GET only reads, DELETE removes, PUT replaces fully). This should be something the candidate can say out loud. 356 - **Key request fields** (NOT a full schema — just the 2-4 fields that matter most): `fieldName: type` — one line WHY it exists (e.g. "`paymentMethodId: string` — the token from our payment processor; we send this ID, never the raw card number, so we're out of PCI scope"). If a field is intentionally ABSENT (e.g. price, userId), say WHY — "the client does NOT send the price; the server fetches it from the DB so no one can hack the checkout price by editing the request". 357 - **Concrete example** — write a small JSON block showing a real example request body (for POST/PUT) and the corresponding response body. Use realistic values, not "string" placeholders. For GET endpoints, show the query parameters as a URL + the response JSON. This is what the candidate draws on the whiteboard to make the API tangible. Example format: 358 ``` 359 // Request 360 POST /v1/orders 361 { "paymentMethodId": "pm_abc123", "cartId": "cart_xyz789" } 362 363 // Response 200 364 { "orderId": "ord_def456", "status": "PENDING", "estimatedAt": "2025-01-15T14:32:00Z" } 365 ``` 366 Include only the fields that matter; omit verbose boilerplate. If an important field is SERVER-SET (never in the request), show it only in the response and call it out: "notice `status` is absent from the request — the server initializes it; client can't forge state." 367 - **One key design decision** on this endpoint — the thing you'd call out in an interview: idempotency key, why PENDING not synchronous, why a redirect vs a JSON response, etc. State it in plain words. 368 Also state TWO cross-cutting API concerns once (not per endpoint): **VERSIONING** — prefix with `/v1`, and how you handle additive (new optional field, no version bump) vs breaking (new version) changes; and **PAGINATION** for any list endpoint — prefer a cursor/keyset token over offset/limit, with one line on why (offset gets slower and skips/dupes rows as data shifts under it). 369 Default REST unless there's a reason not to; call out and justify non-obvious choices. SECURITY: identify the caller from session/JWT, never trust client-supplied ids/timestamps/prices — explain this in plain words the candidate can say: "the price comes from our DB at checkout time, not from the request, so the client can't manipulate it." Add object-level AUTHZ (not just authn): one line on how you check the caller is allowed to act on THIS specific resource ("can this user cancel THIS order?"). 💬 note + a 🎙️ Script walking the endpoints. 370 371 ## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time) 372 Build ONE subsection per above-the-line requirement from §1 — cover ALL of them, in priority order, start minimal, don't jump to scaling. CRITICAL: every §6.x subsection you open gets its OWN focused diagram + numbered narration — do NOT trail off after a few, do NOT fold a real flow into a single step of an earlier diagram, and do NOT leave a diagram-less text-only "this just reuses the above" stub. If a flow mostly reuses existing components, STILL draw the cumulative diagram and highlight the new edges/state it adds (e.g. the accept/decline transition, the navigation/track stream). A flow either earns a full diagrammed subsection here or it stays out of §6 — never a half-baked one. For EACH requirement, a subsection: 373 ### 6.x "" 374 - A one-sentence framing of this slice — what the user is trying to do and why this flow is interesting. 375 - Introduce ONLY the new components this slice needs, wired onto what already exists. 376 377 **ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE** — For EACH new component, write FIVE columns: **Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept** The "What it is" column must define the technology in one plain sentence as if explaining to a smart non-engineer — NO jargon without definition. The "Why THIS choice" column must name the specific reason this problem needs this tool (not generic "it scales" — something precise like "ES because we need ranked full-text search across 100M products in <100ms — no SQL can do that"). The "What we DIDN'T pick" column names 1-2 realistic alternatives and one concrete reason each was rejected (e.g. "Not SQL LIKE: would scan every product row on every search — 100M rows × every query = unusable"; "Not a custom inverted index: months of engineering with no relevance ranking"). Keep this to 2–5 rows per slice. Example format: 378 | Search index | Elasticsearch — a search engine that pre-builds a map of words → products, so a query returns matches in ms | Need ranked full-text search across 100M products in under 100ms | Not SQL LIKE (scans every row, no ranking); not a hand-rolled index (months of work) | Lags behind catalog DB by a few seconds (fine for display) | 379 THEN: a 🎙️ NARRATION block (not the table — this is what the candidate says OUT LOUD) that speaks each choice conversationally: define the technology briefly, say WHY you picked it, say what you considered instead and discarded, and state the trade-off you accepted. One short paragraph per component. The candidate reads this and should be able to say it naturally without memorizing jargon. 380 381 **GLOSSES BLOCK** — After the table, write a dedicated "**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**" block. For EVERY technical term used in the table or this section (a service name, a pattern name, a protocol), write it as a standalone bolded entry with a plain-English definition + a one-line "why it matters here" sentence. Format: 382 > **Elasticsearch** — [what it is in one simple sentence]. Here we use it because [one line]. 383 > **CDC (Change Data Capture)** — [what it is]. Here we use it because [one line]. 384 > **Kafka** — [what it is]. Here we use it because [one line]. 385 Each entry must be self-contained — the candidate can read just that entry and understand the term. Never bundle multiple terms into one paragraph; each gets its own line. 386 387 **DIAGRAM** — Include a diagram for this slice ONLY if it adds clarity that text alone cannot provide (a new component, a non-obvious data path, a branching flow). If this slice only adds one edge to existing components already fully drawn in a previous subsection, SKIP the diagram and write "↳ builds on §6.x diagram — no new components" instead. When you DO include one: 388 - Use ` ```mermaid flowchart TD ` (top-down) by DEFAULT — this keeps the diagram narrow enough to fit a 13-inch MacBook Air screen without horizontal scrolling. Switch to `flowchart LR` ONLY for a dead-simple 3-4 node linear sequence (A→B→C, no branching) where vertical space would be wasted. 389 - Use `subgraph` blocks to separate conceptually distinct paths (e.g. subgraph "Read Path" vs subgraph "Write / Sync Path"), with a readable title label on each subgraph. 390 - Keep FOCUSED — only the components and edges this flow touches (~8-12 nodes total). 391 - Color palette strictly: clients (blue #4A90D9 fill), our services (green #2E7D4F fill), async/queues (amber #B7791F fill), datastores as database[] cylinders (grey #4A5568 fill), external (purple #6B46C1 fill). ALL node fills must be dark enough to contrast with white text. 392 - EVERY edge must have a label — not just a number, but a short description: `-->|"1: GET /search"|` not just `-->|1|`. Include WHAT is being sent or what the operation is. 393 - Number the request arrows sequentially 1:1 with the prose narration below. 394 - Add a one-line caption above the diagram (bold) naming the flow. 395 - Directly below the diagram, a one-line `↳ reuses existing: (from §6.x)` note. 396 397 **NUMBERED STEP NARRATION** — Walk through each numbered arrow in the diagram. For EACH step write: 398 1. What happens (the action) — in a complete sentence. 399 2. WHY this component handles it (not just "it goes there" but "because this service owns X"). 400 3. What STATE changes (what is different in the system after this step vs before). 401 4. What could go wrong here and how the system handles it (one line — the "what if" every interviewer asks about). 402 This is the richest part of each slice. The candidate should be able to narrate every arrow on the diagram confidently. 403 404 **💬 Say-while-drawing line** — one sentence that captures the KEY insight of this slice, phrased to say out loud while pointing at the diagram. Should name the core tension and the design decision that resolves it. 405 406 **🎙️ Script** — 3–5 connected sentences narrating this slice end-to-end in plain English. Should sound like someone explaining to a colleague, not reading a textbook. Must: define any jargon the first time it appears, state the one architectural decision that makes this slice work, name the trade-off and why it's acceptable. 407 408 End with the ONE full diagram captioned "Final (high-level)" — it MUST be the exact RECONCILED UNION of all the slices: EVERY component that appeared in ANY slice (6.1, 6.2, …) is present here with its same name and color (NOTHING dropped — never lose a cache, DB, or queue that a slice introduced), and it introduces NOTHING that wasn't built in a slice. Use subgraphs in the final diagram to group the "Read Path" components vs "Write / Transaction Path" vs "Async / Events" path so the visual tells the story at a glance. Before finalizing, mentally diff it against the slices and confirm nothing is missing. Annotate load-bearing schema columns inline next to the relevant datastore, then a 🎙️ Script narrating the full flow (2–3 sentences per path, not one blob) and a 🤝 Checkpoint handing the deep-dive choice to the interviewer. 409 410 ## 7. Data Model & Storage 411 **PART A — ENTITY TABLE:** For each entity, one row with: Entity | Key fields (name + type, annotate the PK/shard key) | Chosen store | Shard/partition key | Consistency guarantee Keep field lists tight — only the columns that matter for access patterns or the deep dives. 412 413 **PART B — STORAGE DECISION CARDS:** After the table, for EACH distinct storage choice (each different database/store used), write a standalone "decision card" block. Each card has FIVE parts: 414 415 **🗄️ [Store name] — used for: [entities]** 416 1. **What it is (plain words):** one sentence defining this store as if explaining to a non-engineer. (e.g. "PostgreSQL is a relational database — it stores data in rows and columns with strict rules, and it guarantees that a multi-step operation either fully succeeds or fully rolls back.") 417 2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern that forces this choice:** Be specific — name the EXACT reason this problem needs THIS store. Not "it scales" but "we need an atomic 'decrement-and-check' operation that prevents overselling, and only a relational DB with row-level locking can guarantee that two buyers racing for the last unit can't both win." 418 3. **What we considered instead and why we rejected each:** List 2-3 realistic alternatives with a one-line rejection reason each. Make the rejections concrete — not "it doesn't scale" but "DynamoDB would work for the cart but NOT for inventory — it can't do the conditional atomic decrement we need to prevent overselling without a lot of custom application-side logic." E.g.: 419 - **MongoDB** — flexible schema is nice, but we need ACID multi-row transactions for the order+inventory+payment saga; Mongo's transactions are slower and less battle-tested here. 420 - **DynamoDB** — great for carts (KV by user) but no native conditional decrement strong enough for inventory; also vendor lock-in. 421 - **MySQL** — identical to Postgres for this use case; Postgres has better JSON support and is the standard choice in modern stacks. 422 4. **Trade-off we accept:** one line on what we're giving up (e.g. "operational complexity of managing shards; query joins across shards are impossible so we denormalize"). 423 5. **🗣️ How to say it out loud:** 2-3 natural sentences the candidate can say verbatim — casual, not textbook. (e.g. "For orders and inventory I'm using Postgres, because this is money and I can't afford a half-committed transaction. I looked at Dynamo — it's great for the cart — but it can't do the atomic stock decrement I need without extra complexity. The trade-off is I have to shard manually, but that's fine because I shard by order_id and each shard is independent.") 424 425 **PART C — Per-operation consistency summary:** one compact table: Operation | Store | Consistency level | Why that level is right here Cover: the writes that MUST be strong (money, inventory), the reads that can be eventual (display, search), and the read-your-own-writes edge case. 426 427 **PART D — DATA LIFECYCLE:** For the LARGEST / fastest-growing table (the one from rigor invariant 3 — usually events/analytics/logs), state the retention window and tie it back to the §3 monthly storage $ figure ("at ~50TB/month, 90-day retention caps us at ~150TB — without it we grow unbounded"). Name the reclamation mechanism: time-partitioned tables with a PARTITION DROP (O(1), instant) rather than row-by-row DELETE (which churns the index and never reclaims space cleanly), plus where cold data goes (S3/Glacier archival tier) if it must be kept. One line is enough; the point is showing the big table has an answer to "then what?". 428 429 **🎙️ Script:** 4-6 sentences walking the whole storage tier conversationally — mention the dominant choice (usually Postgres for the core), the KV store, the cache, and the event log. Must name one "I considered X but rejected it because Y" to signal senior-level thinking. 430 431 ## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great (the senior signal) 432 This is where the interview is won. Start with a one-line 🆘 "If you get stuck" recovery pointer. Pick the 3–5 hardest problems (driven by the non-functional requirements and edge cases). Pose EACH as a question header: **"### How do we [problem]?"** 433 434 For EACH deep dive, escalate through tiers. Each tier has a FIXED structure — follow it exactly: 435 436 **#### [Bad / Good / Great]: ** 437 438 *(For Good and Great tiers only)* **↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** Open with one sentence saying exactly what broke in the tier before and WHY this new approach fixes that specific thing. This makes the progression feel like logical problem-solving, not a random list of techniques. E.g. for Good after Bad: "Bad held the lock across the payment call — so I split it: reserve first in a millisecond, pay second, outside any lock." Never skip this line in Good or Great. 439 440 **🗣️ What this is in plain words:** One sentence explaining the approach as if to a smart non-engineer who has never heard of it. No jargon without definition. This line comes FIRST, before any technical description, so the candidate understands what they're about to describe. 441 442 **Approach:** Explain what you actually do in this tier — the mechanism. Be specific about the steps. Every technical term used here must be defined inline the FIRST time it appears in this deep dive (not just in the glosses — right here, parenthetically: e.g. "a row lock (a database mechanism that prevents any other transaction from reading or writing that row until you release it)"). 443 444 **Why this seemed reasonable / Why people try this:** One line — the intuition behind this approach. The candidate should be able to say "I tried X first because it felt natural — it's what most people reach for." 445 446 **⚠️ What breaks (Challenges):** Be concrete — not "it doesn't scale" but "at 500K buyers racing for one item during a flash sale, the lock is held across a payment call that takes 300ms, so 500K threads are now waiting — the database connection pool (the fixed set of open connections the app keeps — typically 50-200) exhausts in seconds, and the whole site stops responding." Name the numbers. Name the failure mode. Name why this matters for THIS system. 447 448 **🔁 What forces the upgrade to the next tier:** one sentence — what exact scenario makes this tier break that the next tier fixes. (Omit on the final Great tier — replace with the failure matrix or trade-offs instead.) 449 450 Tiers are OPTIONAL — skip Bad if the answer is obvious; don't manufacture tiers. For EACH MEANINGFUL tier (usually Good and Great), include a titled mermaid diagram showing ONLY the mechanism for that tier — not the whole system. For the Great tier also include: 451 452 **🔢 Decision-forcing math (if applicable):** Show a quick calculation that proves why this tier matters — e.g. "500K buyers × 300ms lock hold ÷ 200 connection pool = exhaustion in under 1 second." Keep it rounded and talkable. 453 454 **✅ Failure matrix (for correctness-critical deep dives):** A compact table of every failure scenario and what happens — e.g. "Payment succeeds but commit crashes → reconcile by idempotency key → complete commit, no double-charge." Cover: success, failure, timeout, retry, partial failure, crash-after-payment. 455 456 Each deep dive ends with: 457 - **🎙️ Script:** 3-5 connected sentences saying the Great solution out loud, in plain conversational English. Define every term the first time. Sound like an engineer explaining to a colleague, not reading slides. 458 - **🧠 "If they ask…":** the single most obvious pushback and a 2-sentence answer. 459 460 Cover where relevant: the binding bottleneck + its relief + the new ceiling after the fix; hot-key / celebrity-item problems; idempotency and duplicate suppression; every global coordinator (anything that is a single point of serialization); consistency stance (strong vs eventual) and read-your-own-writes; what happens when each piece fails. 461 462 ## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost 463 Structure this as FOUR named sub-sections, each with plain-English definitions for every term: 464 465 **9A — Availability targets (what the "nines" mean):** 466 For each path (read path, money/write path), state the availability target as "X nines" AND what that means in human terms: "four nines = 52 minutes of downtime per year total; three nines = 8.7 hours." Then state the mechanisms that achieve it (CDN, replicas, multi-AZ failover). Make the mechanism readable: define "multi-AZ" (multiple data centres in the same region so if one building loses power the other takes over automatically) the first time it appears. 467 468 **9B — Per-dependency failure table:** 469 For EACH major component, one row: Component | What breaks if it goes down | How the system degrades gracefully | Recovery action. The "graceful degradation" column must describe what the USER SEES — not just "falls back to replica" but "users can still browse categories but search results might be slightly stale." Define every fallback mechanism inline (e.g. "serve from Redis cache (the in-memory store) until the search cluster recovers"). 470 471 **9C — RPO / RTO per data class (plain definitions FIRST):** 472 Define RPO and RTO in plain words before using them: "RPO (Recovery Point Objective) = how much data we're OK losing if a crash happens right now. RPO≈0 means we lose nothing — every write is synchronously replicated before we acknowledge it. RTO (Recovery Time Objective) = how long before the service is working again after a crash." Then give a table: Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism. Cover: orders/payments (RPO≈0), catalog (looser — can rebuild from source), search index (rebuildable, minutes of lag OK). 473 474 **9D — Cost breakdown:** 475 Name the top 3 cost drivers and WHY each is expensive in plain terms (e.g. "CDN egress — every byte of image data served to a user's browser is charged by the CDN; at millions of page views a day this is the biggest line item, not the database"). Give a rough monthly order-of-magnitude. Name the one optimization that would cut cost the most. 476 477 **🎙️ Script:** 3-4 sentences on the failure story — what the user EXPERIENCES when each major thing fails, not just what the system does internally. 478 479 ## 10. Trade-off Ledger 480 For EACH of the 2–4 most significant design decisions, write a DECISION CARD with five parts: 481 482 **🔀 Decision: [what you chose] vs [what you didn't]** 483 1. **What we chose and why:** one line — the core reason. 484 2. **What we gave up:** one concrete thing that's harder or worse because of this choice. 485 3. **🗣️ Plain words:** one sentence a non-engineer can understand — "think of it like choosing a fast highway that's slightly less reliable over a slower but guaranteed road." 486 4. **When this reverses:** the exact condition that would make you flip this decision — be specific ("if flash sales are removed from scope, we drop the Redis counter and go back to a single Postgres row — simpler, no reconciliation needed"; "if we owned the payment processor in-house, we could do a real two-phase commit instead of a saga"). 487 5. **🗣️ How to say it:** one sentence the candidate can say out loud — natural, not textbook. 488 489 Tie each decision back to the non-functional requirements and any "⚠️ Gaps" from §1. **🎙️ Script:** 3-4 sentences walking the ledger — the decisions, the trade-offs, and what would make you change your mind. 490 491 ## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers 492 Cover ALL of these question domains — generate 2-3 questions per domain, 12-18 total: (1) Core algorithm / the hardest part of THIS specific problem (e.g. "two buyers race for the last unit — who wins?"), (2) Failure scenarios ("what if the payment processor goes down?", "what if Redis crashes?"), (3) Scale / hot-spot ("how do you handle a flash sale?"), (4) Consistency / correctness ("can the same order be placed twice?", "can you oversell?"), (5) Security ("how do you stop price tampering?", "what if someone forges a user id?"), (6) Cost ("what's the most expensive part?", "how would you cut costs?"), (7) Extensibility ("how would you add feature X?", "how would you support Y?"). Do NOT generate only checkout/core questions — a real interview covers all domains. 493 494 For EACH question, write FOUR parts: 495 496 **❓ [The question exactly as an interviewer would say it]** 497 498 **The mechanism (what actually happens):** 2-3 sentences. Every term used that hasn't been defined earlier must be defined inline here. 499 500 **🗣️ In plain words:** 1-2 sentences — same answer, zero jargon. This is what the candidate says if they blank on the technical version: "In simple terms, [plain version]." 501 502 **💬 One-liner to say out loud:** the sharpest single sentence — confident, not textbook. 503 504 Answers must be REAL and SPECIFIC — not "we use caching" but "the product page is in Redis with a 60-second TTL; during a flash sale the cache absorbs ~8K requests/s so Postgres never sees the spike." No one-liners as the main answer. Mechanism + plain words + one-liner every time. 505 506 End with a **🎙️ "60-second verbal summary"** — follow this template exactly, filling in the specifics for this problem: "[1 sentence: what the system is in plain words]. The design splits into two halves. [2-3 sentences: the READ HALF — what it is, what technologies, why it works]. [2-3 sentences: the WRITE / TRANSACTION HALF — what it is, the hardest problem in it, and the one mechanism that solves it]. [1 sentence: how the two halves connect — usually an event stream or async pipeline]. [1 sentence: the single design principle that every hard decision traces back to]." Write this as flowing connected sentences — no bullet points, no section headers. It should sound like someone giving a confident wrap-up to a colleague, not reading notes. 507 508

content.text: I've read both prompt files. Now let me set up the workspace and write the capacity.py file to compute the numbers I'll need.

input.description: Create workspace dir and verify venv python exists

content.text: Now let me write the capacity.py to compute the talkable numbers for this real-time restaurant order metrics system.

input.content:
"""
Capacity model for: Real-Time Restaurant Order Metrics (Food Delivery)
Live operational dashboard: orders/min, prep times, late orders per restaurant/region.
We surface ONLY rounded, talkable numbers. Every chain is printed so the candidate
can explain HOW each number was derived out loud.
"""

def r(n):
    """Round to a talkable phrase."""
    if n >= 1_000_000_000:
        return f"~{n/1_000_000_000:.1f}B"
    if n >= 1_000_000:
        return f"~{n/1_000_000:.1f}M"
    if n >= 1_000:
        return f"~{n/1_000:.0f}K"
    return f"~{n:.0f}"

print("=" * 70)
print("REAL-TIME RESTAURANT ORDER METRICS — CAPACITY MODEL")
print("=" * 70)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 0. Locked assumptions (from §1 scope lock)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
restaurants          = 1_000_000      # active restaurants on the platform
orders_per_day       = 60_000_000     # 60M orders/day platform-wide (DoorDash-scale)
SECONDS_PER_DAY      = 86_400
events_per_order     = 8              # lifecycle events: created, accepted, prepping,
                                      # ready, picked-up, en-route, delivered, +1 status
burst_factor         = 5             # peak/avg multiplier (lunch + dinner double rush,
                                      # Friday night, weather spikes)
print(f"\n[assumptions] {r(restaurants)} restaurants, {r(orders_per_day)} orders/day, "
      f"{events_per_order} events/order, burst x{burst_factor}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 1. INGEST: event firehose (the write/streaming side — the heart of this system)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
events_per_day = orders_per_day * events_per_order
avg_events_qps = events_per_day / SECONDS_PER_DAY
peak_events_qps = avg_events_qps * burst_factor
print("\n--- 1. INGEST EVENT FIREHOSE ---")
print(f"events/day      = {orders_per_day:,} orders x {events_per_order} = {events_per_day:,}")
print(f"avg events/s    = {events_per_day:,} / {SECONDS_PER_DAY:,} = {avg_events_qps:,.0f}/s  -> {r(avg_events_qps)}/s")
print(f"PEAK events/s   = {avg_events_qps:,.0f} x {burst_factor} = {peak_events_qps:,.0f}/s -> {r(peak_events_qps)}/s")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 2. DASHBOARD READS: ops users polling / streaming the live board
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
ops_users          = 50_000          # ops/regional managers + restaurant owners watching live
refresh_seconds    = 5               # board refresh cadence (push or 5s poll)
avg_read_qps       = ops_users / refresh_seconds
peak_read_qps      = avg_read_qps * burst_factor
print("\n--- 2. DASHBOARD READ QPS ---")
print(f"avg reads/s     = {ops_users:,} viewers / {refresh_seconds}s refresh = {avg_read_qps:,.0f}/s -> {r(avg_read_qps)}/s")
print(f"PEAK reads/s    = {avg_read_qps:,.0f} x {burst_factor} = {peak_read_qps:,.0f}/s -> {r(peak_read_qps)}/s")

# read:write ratio (this system is WRITE-heavy — unusual, drives the whole design)
ratio_write_to_read = peak_events_qps / peak_read_qps
print(f"WRITE:READ      = {peak_events_qps:,.0f} : {peak_read_qps:,.0f} = ~{ratio_write_to_read:.0f}:1 (WRITE-heavy)")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 3. THE DECISION-FORCING NUMBER: can one Kafka partition / one stateful
#    stream-processor task absorb the peak per-key event rate?
#    Hot key = a single huge restaurant (stadium/airport ghost-kitchen) OR a
#    single dense region shard at dinner peak.
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print("\n--- 3. DECISION-FORCING NUMBER: hot-shard event rate ---")
# Aggregation is keyed by restaurant_id and rolled up to region. A region shard
# concentrates traffic. Suppose top region = 2% of national volume at peak.
top_region_share   = 0.02
region_peak_qps    = peak_events_qps * top_region_share
# One stateful stream task (Flink keyed operator) comfortably handles ~? events/s
# when it must do windowed stateful aggregation w/ RocksDB state (not a stateless map).
stateful_task_ceiling = 50_000       # ~50K events/s per stateful task is a safe ceiling
tasks_needed       = region_peak_qps / stateful_task_ceiling
print(f"top region peak = {peak_events_qps:,.0f} x {top_region_share:.0%} = {region_peak_qps:,.0f}/s -> {r(region_peak_qps)}/s")
print(f"stateful task ceiling ~ {stateful_task_ceiling:,}/s")
print(f"=> tasks for hot region = {region_peak_qps:,.0f} / {stateful_task_ceiling:,} = {tasks_needed:.1f} -> need parallelism")
# FLIP THRESHOLD: when does a single keyed task stop coping?
flip_threshold = stateful_task_ceiling
print(f"FLIP: a single keyed restaurant exceeding ~{r(flip_threshold)}/s events can't fit one task")
print(f"      -> must pre-aggregate at the edge (combiner) before the keyed shuffle")

# Kafka partition sizing for ingest
kafka_partition_ceiling = 10_000     # safe sustained events/s per partition incl. headroom
partitions_needed = peak_events_qps / kafka_partition_ceiling
print(f"\nKafka partitions = {peak_events_qps:,.0f} / {kafka_partition_ceiling:,} per-partition "
      f"= {partitions_needed:.0f} -> round to ~{int(round(partitions_needed/100.0))*100} partitions")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 4. TIME-SERIES STORAGE FOOTPRINT (the largest table = pre-aggregated rollups)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

bytes_per_point    = 32              # ts(8)+value(8)+tags-overhead amortized in a TSDB
minutes_per_day    = 1440

raw_bytes_per_day  = points_per_day * bytes_per_point

      f"= {points_per_day:,} pts/day -> {r(points_per_day)} pts/day")
print(f"raw bytes/day   = {points_per_day:,} x {bytes_per_point}B = {raw_bytes_per_day/1e12:.2f} TB/day")

# S3 cold + misc

total_cost = flink_cost + kafka_cost + tsdb_cost + s3_cold_cost

print(f"Kafka/MSK            = {kafka_brokers} brokers x ${cost_per_broker_mo} = ${kafka_cost:,}/mo")
print(f"TSDB serving         = {tsdb_nodes} nodes x ${cost_per_tsdb_mo} = ${tsdb_cost:,}/mo")

print(f"TOTAL                ~ ${total_cost:,}/mo -> {r(total_cost)}/mo")

# unit economics: $ per 1M events processed
events_per_month   = events_per_day * 30
cost_per_million_events = total_cost / (events_per_month / 1_000_000)
print(f"\nunit econ: events/mo = {events_per_month:,} ; cost/1M events = "
      f"${cost_per_million_events:.3f} -> ~${cost_per_million_events:.2f} per 1M events")

print("\n" + "=" * 70)

input.content:
# Design Real-Time Restaurant Order Metrics (Food Delivery)

> **The ask:** a live operational dashboard for a food-delivery platform — orders per minute, prep times, and late orders, per restaurant and per region, updated in near-real-time so ops teams can react while it's still happening.

---

## 1. Scope, Requirements & Assumptions

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock scope with a couple of quick assumptions, state the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one requirement at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — the streaming aggregation pipeline, exactly-once counting, and late events — checking in with you as I go. Sound good?"

The core tension in one line: this isn't a "store and query" system, it's a **streaming aggregation** system — a firehose of order-lifecycle events comes in, and ops people want to *see the present* (orders/min right now, are we running late right now) within a few seconds. Working backwards from the customer (the ops/regional manager): they need to spot a struggling region during the dinner rush and act *before* the bad orders pile up. So freshness and the cost of running the pipeline 24/7 are the two things that will drive every decision.

### 🔒 Scope lock (stated as vetoable assumptions — stop me if any is wrong)

1. **This is an OPERATIONAL dashboard, not a financial system of record.** I'll assume "near-real-time and approximately correct" beats "perfectly exact but minutes late." Counts can be momentarily off by a hair as late events settle, as long as they converge. *(If you actually want billing-grade exact counts on the live path, that forks the design — I'd add a stronger exactly-once story and slow the freshness SLA.)*
2. **Aggregation is keyed by `restaurant_id`, rolled up to `region_id`.** I'll assume those are the two grouping levels ops cares about. *(If you also need per-courier or per-menu-item live rollups, that multiplies the keyspace and the state size — flag it and I'll add a dimension.)*
3. **Near-real-time means single-digit seconds, not sub-100ms.** I'll assume a freshness target of **~5 seconds end to end** (event happens → visible on the board). *(If you need sub-second, we're in a much more expensive low-latency streaming regime; if a 1-minute lag is fine, this gets much cheaper.)*
4. **Multi-region for availability, but a dashboard read is region-local.** I'll assume the platform is global but an ops user watches their own region, so we don't need globally-consistent cross-region aggregates on the hot path. *(If a single global "all regions" live number must be strongly consistent, that adds a global coordinator.)*

> 🤝 **In / out of scope — add or cut before I commit?** In: ingesting order-lifecycle events, windowed aggregation into rollups, serving the live board, handling late and out-of-order events, exactly-once *counting*. Out: the ordering/checkout system itself, courier dispatch, the consumer app, ML demand forecasting, and long-horizon BI/warehouse analytics (that's a nightly batch concern, not this real-time board).

### Functional requirements (the core flows — "above the line")

Since we assumed an operational, restaurant-and-region-keyed, ~5-second board, the core flows are:

**FR1 — Ingest order-lifecycle events.** The system ingests a firehose of events (order created, accepted, prepping, ready, picked up, delivered, plus courier location pings and prep heartbeats) from the order service.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** every time something happens to an order anywhere on the platform, a little note about it lands in our pipeline.
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** it's ~200K events/second at peak with bursts, events arrive out of order and sometimes twice, and we can't lose them.

**FR2 — Compute windowed rollups (orders/min, prep time, late orders).** Continuously aggregate the firehose into per-restaurant and per-region metrics over rolling time windows.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** turn the raw stream of "this happened" notes into running scoreboards like "this restaurant did 14 orders in the last minute and is averaging 22-minute prep."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** a "late order" is defined by comparing event timestamps to promised times across a window, and counts must be *exactly once* — an event counted twice corrupts the scoreboard.

**FR3 — Serve the live dashboard (per restaurant / per region).** Ops users query and watch the current metrics, sliced by restaurant or region, refreshed every few seconds.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** an ops manager opens the board and sees their region's live numbers updating by themselves.
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** ~60K reads/second at peak, each wanting the *freshest* rollup, without hammering the thing the pipeline is writing to.

**FR4 — Handle late and out-of-order events correctly.** Events that arrive after their window "closed" (a delayed delivery confirmation, a phone that buffered location pings offline) must still update the right historical minute.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** if the news about an order shows up a little late, we still fix the right minute's number instead of dropping it or sticking it in the wrong bucket.
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** you can't keep every window open forever (memory), but closing too early loses accuracy — this is the watermark/allowed-lateness trade-off.

**Below the line (out of scope for this pass):** the checkout/order-placement system, courier dispatch & ETA prediction, the consumer-facing app, alerting/anomaly-detection (a natural follow-on but a separate component), nightly warehouse/BI analytics, and historical multi-year reporting.

### Non-Functional requirements (PROVISIONAL targets — tagged to assumptions)

**NFR1 — Freshness ~5s p95, end to end.** (depends on assumption 3)
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** something that happens now shows up on the board within about five seconds, almost always.
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** ops reacts to a problem that's already 5 minutes old — the rush is over and orders are already late; the dashboard is a rear-view mirror, useless for operations.

**NFR2 — Ingest never drops events; counting is exactly-once.** (depends on assumption 1)
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** we never lose a note, and we never count the same thing twice.
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** orders/min drifts wrong over the day, "late orders" undercounts, and ops loses trust in the board — once they stop trusting a number they stop looking at it.

**NFR3 — Read availability 99.9% on the dashboard, 99.99% on ingest.** (depends on assumption 4)
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** the board is down under ~9 hours a year; the ingest pipe almost never (under ~1 hour a year) because dropping events is worse than a blank screen.
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** if ingest goes down, the gap is permanent — those minutes are blind forever even after recovery, unlike a read outage which heals.

**NFR4 — Cost-efficient and operable 24/7.** (Amazon lens — cost & ops first-class)
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** running this around the clock shouldn't cost a fortune, and on-call shouldn't be paged every night.
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** a streaming cluster running 24/7 quietly becomes one of the biggest line items on the bill, and a fragile pipeline burns out the on-call.

> 💬 "The thing to notice up front is this is a *write-heavy* system — about three events in for every dashboard read out — which is the opposite of most designs. So I'll spend my budget on the ingest-and-aggregate path, not on a read cache."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "So this is a live ops dashboard for a food-delivery platform — orders per minute, prep times, late orders, per restaurant and region, fresh within about five seconds. I'm treating it as operational and approximately-correct rather than billing-exact, keyed by restaurant rolled up to region, and region-local on reads. The four core flows are: ingest the event firehose, aggregate it into windowed rollups, serve the live board, and handle late or out-of-order events correctly. The non-functionals that matter most are freshness, never losing events with exactly-once counting, and keeping a 24/7 streaming cluster affordable. The one thing I want to flag is it's unusually write-heavy, so the design center of gravity is the streaming pipeline, not a read cache."

---

## 2. The Forks Behind My Assumptions (what changes if I'm wrong)

These are the residual forks from the scope lock — the assumptions where, if I picked wrong, the architecture genuinely changes. I'm not re-asking scale; I've committed to ~200K events/s. These are about *shape*.

**Fork A — Approximately-correct vs billing-exact counting.**
- **The assumption I locked:** this is operational, so counts may wobble for a second as late events settle, as long as they converge and are exactly-once over the window.
- ↔️ **What changes if I'm wrong:**
  - If actually **approximately-correct is fine** → I can use a fast probabilistic path and emit early/partial windows, getting freshness cheaply.
  - If actually **billing-exact** → every count needs transactional exactly-once with no early emits; freshness slips from 5s toward 30s+ and cost climbs, because I can't show a window until it's provably sealed.
- 🗣️ **How I'd say it:** "If these numbers ever feed billing or SLA penalties, tell me — I'd tighten exactly-once and trade away some freshness."

**Fork B — Region-local reads vs one strongly-consistent global number.**
- **The assumption I locked:** an ops user watches their own region, so I aggregate per region and don't need a globally-consistent total on the hot path.
- ↔️ **What changes if I'm wrong:**
  - If actually **region-local** → each region's pipeline is independent, no cross-region coordination, easy to scale and isolate failures.
  - If actually **one live global total that must be strongly consistent** → I need a global aggregation layer that sums region rollups, which is a global coordinator with its own throughput ceiling and partition behavior — a real cost.
- 🗣️ **How I'd say it:** "Is there a single 'whole platform right now' number that has to be exactly right live, or is region-by-region good enough? That changes whether I need a global rollup tier."

**Fork C — How late is "too late" for a late event.**
- **The assumption I locked:** events can lag by up to a few minutes (a phone buffering pings offline) and still count toward the right minute.
- ↔️ **What changes if I'm wrong:**
  - If actually **lateness is bounded to seconds** → small allowed-lateness, windows close fast, state is tiny, freshness is great.
  - If actually **events can be hours late** → I can't hold windows open that long; I need a "fast approximate now + corrected later" two-speed design (a speed layer plus a batch correction layer).
- 🗣️ **How I'd say it:** "What's the worst-case event delay we have to honor — seconds, minutes, or hours? That sets how long I hold windows open and whether I need a slow correction path."

> 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "I'll proceed with operational/approximately-correct, region-local reads, and minutes-scale lateness. If any of those should flip, now's the cheapest moment — want to redirect, or should I size it up?"

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

I deferred the math until now; I'll compute only the few numbers that drive a decision. I ran a small script for correctness; here are the rounded results.

> **QPS** = events or queries per second. **Burst factor** = peak ÷ average (food delivery double-peaks at lunch and dinner). **Rollup** = a pre-computed metric for a window, e.g. "orders in this 1-minute bucket." **TSDB** = time-series database, a store optimized for "metric over time" data.

| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Avg ingest QPS | 60M orders/day x 50 events/order = 3B events/day; 3B ÷ 86,400s ≈ 34,722/s | ~35K/s | "about thirty-five thousand events a second on average" | Need a durable streaming buffer, not direct DB writes |
| **Peak ingest QPS** | 34,722/s x 6 burst = 208,333/s | **~208K/s** | "north of two hundred thousand events a second at peak" | **THE binding number — sizes the whole pipeline** |
| Peak dashboard reads | 50K viewers ÷ 5s refresh = 10K/s; x 6 burst = 60K/s | ~60K/s | "about sixty thousand reads a second at peak" | Reads served from a TSDB the pipeline writes — don't double-compute |
| Write:read ratio | 208K writes : 60K reads | ~3:1 | "roughly three writes for every read" | Spend budget on ingest+aggregate, NOT a read cache |
| Hot region peak | 208K/s x 8% (densest metro) = 16,667/s | ~17K/s | "the hottest region pushes about seventeen thousand a second" | Region shards must run in parallel, not one task |
| **Flip threshold** | one stateful aggregation task tops out ~50K/s | **~50K/s per key** | "a single key above fifty thousand a second can't fit one task" | **Above this → pre-aggregate at the edge before the shuffle** |
| Kafka partitions | 208K/s ÷ 10K/s safe-per-partition ≈ 21 → round up | ~32 | "about thirty-two partitions" | Ingest parallelism + consumer scaling |
| Rollup storage (hot) | 1M restaurants x 6 metrics x 1,440 min/day x 32B = ~0.28 TB/day raw; ÷10 compression x3 replication ≈ 83 GB/day; x 90 days | ~7 TB | "only about seven terabytes of rollups for 90 days" | Storage is a non-issue — TSDB compresses time-series ~10x |
| Raw event log | 3B events/day x 400B | ~1.2 TB/day | "about a terabyte a day of raw events" | Keep 3 days hot in Kafka, archive to S3 for replay |
| Cost (monthly) | Flink ~$72K + Kafka ~$135K + TSDB ~$80K + S3 ~$12K | ~$300K/mo | "roughly three hundred grand a month" | Stream compute + the buffer dominate, not storage |
| **Unit economics** | $300K/mo ÷ 90B events/mo x 1M | **~$3.32 / 1M events** | "about three dollars per million events" | The number to optimize down — drives batching/pre-agg |

**The ONE decision-forcing number:** **~208K events/s at peak.** That's well past what a single process or a single Kafka partition can absorb, so the entire pipeline is partitioned from the front door. The **flip threshold** is **~50K/s per key** — below it a single stateful aggregation task handles a key fine; above it (a stadium ghost-kitchen, a Super Bowl region) I *must* pre-aggregate at the edge before the keyed shuffle, or that one task becomes a hot shard and stalls everything behind it.

Storage is genuinely a non-issue here — pre-aggregated time-series compresses ~10x, so 90 days of rollups for a million restaurants is only ~7 TB. The cost story is the opposite: it's the always-on stream-processing compute and the Kafka buffer, ~$210K of the ~$300K/mo.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The number that drives everything is peak ingest — about two hundred thousand events a second. That's the firehose, and it's roughly three writes for every dashboard read, so this is a write-heavy system, which is unusual. A single aggregation task tops out around fifty thousand a second, so anything hotter than that — a stadium or a dense metro on a rainy Friday — has to get pre-aggregated before it reaches the keyed step, otherwise that one shard stalls the pipeline. Storage barely matters because time-series compresses about ten-to-one. What actually costs money is the stream-processing cluster running twenty-four-seven, so that's where I'll optimize."

## 4. Core Entities

A first-draft scaffold — full fields come in §7.

**OrderEvent** — the atomic input: one thing that happened to one order at one time (created, ready, delivered, location ping). Owns: the raw signal and its event-time timestamp.
- 🗣️ **Analogy:** like a single timestamped line in a delivery driver's logbook — "2:14pm, order #88 left the kitchen."
- ⚠️ **Probe:** "How do you know an event's real time vs when it arrived?" → every event carries an *event-time* set at the source; the pipeline trusts event-time, not arrival-time, so late and out-of-order events still land in the right minute.

**Window** — a time bucket (e.g. a 1-minute tumbling window) for one key, accumulating partial aggregates. Owns: the in-flight running counts before they're sealed into a rollup.
- 🗣️ **Analogy:** like a tally sheet for "the 2:14pm minute" that you keep adding marks to until 2:15, then total up.
- ⚠️ **Probe:** "When do you close a window if events can be late?" → on a *watermark* — an estimate of "event-time has advanced past T" — plus a grace period of allowed lateness; after that we seal it.

**Rollup (MetricPoint)** — a sealed, queryable metric for one key + one metric + one time bucket (e.g. restaurant 42, orders, 2:14pm = 14). Owns: the served truth the dashboard reads.
- 🗣️ **Analogy:** the final score posted on the scoreboard for that minute.
- ⚠️ **Probe:** "Restaurant or region — how do you serve both?" → restaurant rollups are the base; region rollups are a second aggregation (sum of restaurants in the region), computed in the same pipeline so both are first-class.

**Restaurant / Region** — the grouping dimensions; mostly static reference data (which region a restaurant is in, its promised prep SLA).
- 🗣️ **Analogy:** the org chart that says which store belongs to which district.
- ⚠️ **Probe:** "A restaurant changes region mid-day — what happens?" → we snapshot the region mapping at event-time so historical rollups don't retroactively move; new events use the new mapping.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The entities are pretty lean: an OrderEvent is one raw thing that happened, a Window is the in-flight tally for a time bucket, and a Rollup is the sealed metric the dashboard actually reads. Restaurant and Region are just grouping dimensions. The one I'd flag is the Window — it's where all the hard parts live, because deciding *when* to close it under late events is the crux of the whole system. I'll add full fields and the time-bucket schema in the data model."

## 5. API / Interface

Two surfaces: an **ingest** interface (internal, high-throughput, from the order service) and a **read** interface (the dashboard). All under `/v1`.

### Ingest — `POST /v1/events:batch` (internal)
- **Why POST:** it creates new state (appends events). Batched because at 200K/s, per-event HTTP would be madness — producers batch hundreds of events per call (or, more realistically, produce straight to Kafka via a client library; this REST shape is for services that can't).
- **Key fields:** `eventId: string` (producer-generated UUID — the **idempotency/dedup key**, so a retried batch can't double-count); `orderId: string`; `type: enum` (CREATED, READY, DELIVERED, ...); `restaurantId: string`; `eventTime: timestamp` (set at the *source*, the basis for windowing — never trust arrival time); `promisedTime: timestamp` (to decide "late"). The server does **not** trust a client-supplied `region` — it looks up region from `restaurantId` at event-time.
- **Concrete:**
```
// Request
POST /v1/events:batch
{ "events": [
  { "eventId": "evt_9f2a", "orderId": "ord_881", "type": "DELIVERED",
    "restaurantId": "rst_42", "eventTime": "2026-06-21T18:14:03Z",
    "promisedTime": "2026-06-21T18:10:00Z" }
] }

// Response 202
{ "accepted": 1, "duplicates": 0 }
```
- **Key decision:** **idempotency via `eventId`** + a dedup store keyed by it with a TTL covering the max lateness window. A retried batch returns 202 with `duplicates` counted but never re-aggregated.

### Read current metrics — `GET /v1/metrics`
- **Why GET:** pure read, cacheable, no state change.
- **Key params:** `?scope=restaurant&id=rst_42&metrics=orders,prepP95,lateCount&window=1m&range=60m`. The client picks the grouping (restaurant or region) and the metrics; the server reads sealed rollups.
- **Concrete:**
```
// GET /v1/metrics?scope=restaurant&id=rst_42&metrics=orders,prepP95,lateCount&window=1m&range=15m

// Response 200
{ "scope": "restaurant", "id": "rst_42", "window": "1m",
  "series": {
    "orders":    [ {"t":"18:13Z","v":12}, {"t":"18:14Z","v":14} ],
    "prepP95":   [ {"t":"18:13Z","v":1380}, {"t":"18:14Z","v":1290} ],
    "lateCount": [ {"t":"18:13Z","v":2},  {"t":"18:14Z","v":3} ]
  },
  "asOf": "18:14:05Z", "watermark": "18:14:00Z" }
```
- **Key decision:** the response carries `asOf` and `watermark` so the UI can honestly show "data is sealed up to 18:14, the current minute is still filling" — read-your-own-writes for a streaming system means telling the user how fresh the truth is.

### Live stream — `GET /v1/metrics:stream` (SSE)
- **Why SSE not poll:** at 50K viewers, server-sent events push deltas instead of 50K clients polling every 5s; one connection per viewer, server pushes a sealed-window update when it lands.
- **Concrete:** `event: rollup\ndata: {"id":"rst_42","t":"18:15Z","orders":15}\n\n`
- **Key decision:** server pushes only on window seal, so push rate is bounded by window count, not by viewer count.

**Cross-cutting (stated once):**
- **Versioning:** `/v1` prefix. Additive change (new optional metric) → no bump. Breaking change (rename a metric, change `window` semantics) → `/v2`.
- **Pagination:** list endpoints (e.g. "all restaurants in region X, sorted by late-rate") use a **cursor/keyset token**, not offset/limit — offset gets slower and skips/dupes rows as live data shifts under it.
- **Security:** caller identity comes from a **JWT** (a signed token proving who's calling), never a client-supplied user id. **Object-level authz:** "can this ops user read region X?" is checked against their assigned regions — a Manhattan manager can't pull Chicago's board. Ingest is mTLS service-to-service only.

> 💬 "Notice the client never sends `region` or `late` — the server derives region from the restaurant and decides 'late' by comparing event-time to promised-time. The client can't forge a region's numbers."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "There are really two APIs. Ingest is a batched POST from the order service, and the key thing is every event carries a producer-generated id that's my dedup key, so retries can't double-count — that's how I get exactly-once. The read side is a GET that returns time-series for a restaurant or region, and crucially it tells you how fresh it is with a watermark, so the UI can honestly mark the current minute as still filling. For the live board I push updates over server-sent events instead of having fifty thousand clients poll. Everywhere, the server derives region and lateness itself — the client can't forge those."

## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time)

### 6.1 "Ingest order-lifecycle events"

The firehose front door: take ~208K events/s, never lose one, and buffer it so a slow downstream consumer can't cause data loss or back-pressure the order service.

**ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ingest Gateway | A thin stateless service that authenticates producers and writes events into the log | Decouples producers from the log, enforces schema + dedup id presence, mTLS | Not direct producer→Kafka for external services (no auth/schema enforcement); not writing to a DB (can't absorb 208K/s) | One extra hop of latency (~ms) |
| Event Log (Kafka) | A durable, append-only log that buffers a firehose so nothing is lost if a consumer is slow | Need to absorb 208K/s, retain 3 days for replay, and let many consumers read independently | Not a queue like SQS (no replay, no ordered partitions, weak per-key ordering); not Kinesis (fine but vendor-locked, similar model) | Operational cost + must size partitions for peak |
| Partitioning by region+restaurant | The log is split into ~32 partitions keyed so one restaurant's events stay ordered together | Per-key ordering is needed so a window sees a restaurant's events in order; spreads load across consumers | Not random partitioning (loses per-key ordering, breaks windowing); not partition-per-restaurant (1M partitions is unmanageable) | A hot key can overload its partition — handled in deep dive |

> 🎙️ **Narration:** "The front door is a thin stateless gateway — it authenticates the order service over mTLS, checks every event has a dedup id and a valid event-time, and appends to a durable log. That log is Kafka — think of it as a tape recorder for events; it holds three days of the firehose so if a downstream consumer falls behind or crashes, nothing is lost and I can replay. I split it into about thirty-two partitions, keyed by region and restaurant so one restaurant's events stay in order, which the windowing step needs. I didn't use a plain queue because I need replay and ordering, and I didn't write straight to a database because no database absorbs two hundred thousand writes a second cheaply."

> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
> **Kafka** — a durable append-only log that buffers a firehose of events and lets multiple consumers read at their own pace; here it holds the order-event stream so nothing's lost if the aggregator is slow.
> **Partition** — one ordered shard of the log; here keying by restaurant keeps a restaurant's events in order so windowed counts are correct.
> **mTLS** — mutual TLS, where both client and server prove identity with certificates; here it authenticates the order service to the ingest gateway so only trusted producers can write.
> **Dedup id** — a unique id the producer stamps on each event; here it lets us drop duplicates so a retried event isn't counted twice.

**Ingest flow**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OS[Order service]
    OS -->|"1 emit events batch"| GW[Ingest gateway]
    subgraph Ingest["Ingest path"]
        GW -->|"2 validate and dedup id"| KAFKA[[Event log Kafka]]
        KAFKA -->|"3 partition by region restaurant"| P1[[Partition shards]]
    end
    GW -.->|"2b reject malformed 400"| OS
    KAFKA -.->|"4 retain 3 days for replay"| S3[(S3 cold archive)]
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc    fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue  fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db     fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class OS client
    class GW svc
    class KAFKA,P1 queue
    class S3 db
```

**NUMBERED STEP NARRATION**
1. **Order service emits a batch.** The order service is the source of truth for order state; it fires events as a side effect of state transitions. *State after:* events in flight. *What could go wrong:* the batch call times out → the producer retries with the same `eventId`s, and step 2's dedup makes the retry safe.
2. **Gateway validates and checks the dedup id.** The gateway owns auth + schema enforcement so the log only ever holds well-formed events. *State after:* valid events appended; malformed ones rejected (2b). *What could go wrong:* gateway overloaded → it returns 429 with Retry-After and producers back off; it never blocks the order service's own DB commit (events are emitted post-commit, fire-and-forget).
3. **Kafka partitions by region+restaurant.** Keying keeps a restaurant's events ordered in one partition. *State after:* durably stored, ordered per key. *What could go wrong:* a hot partition → covered in deep dive 8.2.
4. **Retain 3 days, archive to S3.** Kafka keeps a short hot window; older segments tier to S3 for long replay. *State after:* replayable history. *What could go wrong:* need to reprocess a bad deploy's day → replay from S3.

> 💬 "The whole point of this slice is the log decouples the firehose from the slow part — if my aggregator falls over, events pile up safely in Kafka instead of being lost or back-pressuring the order service."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Ingest is deliberately dumb and bulletproof. A stateless gateway authenticates the order service and appends events to Kafka, a durable log that buffers three days of the firehose. I key partitions by restaurant so events stay ordered, which the aggregation step depends on. The one decision that makes this work is decoupling: by putting a durable log between the firehose and the aggregator, a slow or crashed aggregator never loses data and never pushes back on the order service. The trade-off is the cost of running Kafka at this throughput, which I accept because losing events is the unforgivable failure here."
