### 6.2 "Compute windowed rollups (orders/min, prep time, late orders)"

The heart of the system: a stateful stream processor reads the log, groups by key, accumulates per-minute windows, and emits sealed rollups. This is where exactly-once and watermarks live.

**ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Stream processor (Flink) | An always-on engine that reads the stream, keeps running state per key, and emits results | Native event-time windowing, watermarks, exactly-once via checkpoints, keyed state in RocksDB | Not Spark micro-batch (higher latency, weaker event-time); not hand-rolled consumers (we'd reinvent windows, state, exactly-once badly) | Operationally heavy; stateful jobs are hard to evolve |
| Keyed state (RocksDB) | An embedded on-disk key-value store holding each key's in-flight window tallies | State outgrows memory at 1M keys; RocksDB spills to local disk and is checkpointed | Not in-memory only (OOM at 1M keys + windows); not external DB per-update (too slow per event) | Disk I/O + checkpoint size to manage |
| Two-level aggregation | First aggregate per restaurant, then sum restaurants into region rollups | Both grouping levels are first-class; region = cheap sum of restaurant rollups | Not re-reading raw events per region (duplicates work, 2x cost) | A second pipeline stage + its lag |
| Late-data handling (watermark + allowed lateness) | A rule for when a window is "done" plus a grace period for stragglers | Late events must still update the right minute without holding state forever | Not processing-time windows (wrong buckets under delay); not infinite windows (state explodes) | A bounded staleness/accuracy trade-off |

> 🎙️ **Narration:** "The aggregator is Flink — an always-on stream engine. Think of it as a program that keeps a live tally sheet per restaurant and never stops running. It reads from Kafka, groups events by restaurant, and accumulates one-minute windows. It keeps that state in RocksDB, an embedded on-disk store, because a million restaurants times several open windows won't fit in memory. It does exactly-once with checkpoints — periodic consistent snapshots of all that state plus the Kafka read positions — so on a crash it rewinds to the last snapshot and replays, with no double-counting. I aggregate restaurants first, then just sum them into regions, so I don't re-read the firehose twice. I picked Flink over Spark micro-batch because Spark's higher latency and weaker event-time handling would blow my five-second freshness budget."

> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
> **Flink** — an always-on stream-processing engine with event-time windows and exactly-once; here it turns the raw event stream into per-minute rollups.
> **Watermark** — an estimate that says "event-time has now passed time T"; here it tells Flink when it's safe to close a minute's window.
> **Allowed lateness** — a grace period after the watermark during which late events still update a closed window; here it's how a delayed delivery still counts in the right minute.
> **Checkpoint** — a periodic consistent snapshot of all stream state plus stream read positions; here it's what makes counting exactly-once and crash-recoverable.
> **Tumbling window** — fixed, non-overlapping time buckets (each minute is one); here it's the natural shape for "orders this minute."

**Aggregation flow**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    KAFKA[[Event log Kafka]]
    KAFKA -->|"1 consume by partition"| FLINK[Flink keyed aggregator]
    subgraph Agg["Stateful aggregation"]
        FLINK -->|"2 accumulate window"| STATE[(RocksDB keyed state)]
        FLINK -->|"3 advance watermark"| WM[Watermark tracker]
        STATE -->|"4 seal restaurant rollup"| R1[Restaurant rollups]
        R1 -->|"5 sum into region"| R2[Region rollups]
    end
    FLINK -.->|"6 checkpoint snapshot"| CP[(Checkpoint store S3)]
    R2 -->|"7 write sealed points"| TSDB[(Time series store)]
    R1 -->|"7 write sealed points"| TSDB
    classDef svc    fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue  fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db     fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class KAFKA queue
    class FLINK,WM,R1,R2 svc
    class STATE,CP,TSDB db
```
↳ reuses existing: Event log Kafka (from §6.1)

**NUMBERED STEP NARRATION**
1. **Consume by partition.** Flink assigns partitions to parallel tasks; each task owns a slice of keys. *State after:* events flowing into keyed operators. *What could go wrong:* a task falls behind → consumer lag alert fires (see §9 back-pressure).
2. **Accumulate the window.** For each restaurant+minute, add to running counts (orders++, prep-time samples, late++ if eventTime > promisedTime). *State after:* in-flight tallies in RocksDB. *What could go wrong:* duplicate event → dedup-id check (held in state with TTL) drops it before it's counted.
3. **Advance the watermark.** Flink tracks the max event-time seen, minus a bound, as "time has passed T." *State after:* the watermark moves forward. *What could go wrong:* one stalled source holds the watermark back → idle-source detection lets it advance anyway.
4. **Seal the restaurant rollup.** When watermark passes a window's end + allowed-lateness, the window closes and emits a final point. *State after:* a sealed restaurant rollup; window state freed. *What could go wrong:* an event arrives after seal → goes to a side-output for correction (deep dive 8.3).
5. **Sum into region.** A second keyed stage sums restaurant rollups for the same minute into a region rollup. *State after:* region rollup ready. *What could go wrong:* a restaurant's region changed → mapping snapshotted at event-time.
6. **Checkpoint.** Periodically snapshot all state + Kafka offsets atomically to S3. *State after:* a recoverable point. *What could go wrong:* crash → restore from last checkpoint, replay from saved offsets, exactly-once preserved.
7. **Write sealed points to the TSDB.** Only sealed rollups are written, transactionally tied to the checkpoint (two-phase commit sink) so a replay never double-writes. *State after:* the dashboard can read it. *What could go wrong:* TSDB write fails → retried idempotently on the same (key, minute) so it overwrites, never duplicates.

> 💬 "The magic word here is *event-time* — I bucket by when things actually happened, not when they arrived, and a watermark tells me when it's finally safe to close a minute. That's what makes late events land in the right slot instead of getting dropped or misfiled."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "This is the core. Flink reads the log and keeps a per-restaurant running tally for each one-minute window, in on-disk RocksDB state because a million restaurants won't fit in memory. It uses event-time and a watermark — basically 'time has now passed this point' — to decide when a minute is done, with a short grace period for stragglers. Counting is exactly-once because Flink checkpoints all its state plus its read positions together, so a crash rewinds and replays with no double-counts, and it writes sealed rollups to the time-series store in a two-phase commit so replays never duplicate. I aggregate restaurants first and just sum them into regions, which saves re-reading the firehose. The trade-off is operational weight — stateful streaming jobs are genuinely hard to evolve — but there's no cheaper way to get exactly-once windowed aggregation at this throughput."

### 6.3 "Serve the live dashboard (per restaurant / per region)"

Take the sealed rollups and serve ~60K reads/s and 50K live streams, freshly, without competing with the pipeline's writes.

**ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Time-series store (TSDB) | A database tuned for "metric value over time" with heavy compression and fast range scans | Reads are always "this metric, this key, last N minutes" — a TSDB nails that and compresses ~10x | Not Postgres (range scans over billions of points are slow, no native downsampling); not raw Cassandra (would work but TSDB gives rollup/retention for free) | Less flexible than SQL for ad-hoc joins |
| Query service | A stateless read API that fans out to the TSDB and assembles series | Stateless = scale reads horizontally behind a load balancer | Not querying TSDB directly from clients (no authz, no fan-out control) | Extra hop |
| Live push (SSE hub) | A service holding viewer connections and pushing rollup deltas as they seal | 50K pollers every 5s = 10K/s of redundant work; push only on seal | Not WebSockets (overkill, SSE is simpler one-way); not long-poll (more overhead) | Stateful connections to manage |

> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
> **TSDB (time-series database)** — a store optimized for timestamped metrics, with compression and time-range queries; here it serves "orders for restaurant 42 over the last 15 minutes" in milliseconds.
> **SSE (server-sent events)** — a one-way stream from server to browser over a single HTTP connection; here it pushes live rollup updates to ops dashboards without polling.
> **Downsampling** — collapsing fine-grained points into coarser ones over time (1m → 5m → 1h); here it keeps long ranges fast and storage small.

**Read + live flow**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    UI[Ops dashboard]
    UI -->|"1 GET metrics"| QS[Query service]
    UI -.->|"2 subscribe SSE"| HUB[Live push hub]
    subgraph Read["Read path"]
        QS -->|"3 range read"| TSDB[(Time series store)]
    end
    subgraph Live["Live push path"]
        HUB -->|"5 push on seal"| UI
    end
    TSDB -->|"4 sealed rollups in"| PIPE[Aggregation pipeline]
    PIPE -.->|"4b notify seal"| HUB
```

**NUMBERED STEP NARRATION**
1. **Dashboard issues GET /v1/metrics.** It wants the last N minutes for a key. *State after:* a read in flight. *What could go wrong:* unauthorized region → object-level authz rejects it (§5).
2. **Subscribe over SSE.** For live updates the UI opens one SSE connection. *State after:* viewer registered on the hub. *What could go wrong:* hub node dies → client reconnects to another (connections are stateless beyond the subscription list).
3. **Range read from the TSDB.** The query service does a time-range scan for the key+metrics. *State after:* series returned with an `asOf`/watermark. *What could go wrong:* current minute not sealed yet → returned marked "filling," so the UI shows it as provisional.
4. **Pipeline writes sealed rollups + notifies the hub.** Each seal lands in the TSDB and pings the hub. *State after:* fresh data available + push triggered. *What could go wrong:* hub lags → it pushes best-effort; the TSDB read is the source of truth, push is a convenience.
5. **Hub pushes the delta to viewers.** Only on seal, so push volume scales with window count, not viewer count. *State after:* live boards update. *What could go wrong:* slow client → its buffer fills, hub drops the connection and the client falls back to polling.

> 💬 "Reads come from the same TSDB the pipeline writes — I don't re-aggregate on read. The honesty trick is the watermark: I tell the UI exactly how fresh the data is, so the live number and the 'still filling' minute are clearly distinguished."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The read side is simple because the pipeline already did the work — I just read pre-aggregated rollups out of a time-series store, which is built for 'this metric over the last fifteen minutes' and compresses about ten-to-one. A stateless query service fans out reads, and for the live board I push updates over server-sent events on each window seal, so fifty thousand viewers don't turn into fifty thousand pollers. The key honesty is the watermark in every response: the UI knows which minutes are final and which one is still filling, so nobody mistakes a half-counted current minute for the truth."

### 6.4 "Handle late and out-of-order events correctly"

This flow mostly extends the aggregator, but it adds a real new path — the **side-output correction loop** — so it earns its own diagram.

**ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Allowed-lateness buffer | Keep a closed window's state for a grace period to absorb stragglers | Most late events arrive within minutes; cheap to hold a few minutes of state | Not infinite retention (state explodes at 1M keys); not zero grace (drops real late data) | A bounded accuracy gap for very-late events |
| Side-output stream | A separate output channel for events later than the grace period | Lets us correct old rollups without holding all windows open forever | Not silently dropping (undercounts late orders — the exact metric ops cares about) | A slow correction path with its own lag |
| Idempotent rollup update | A correction overwrites (key, metric, minute) rather than incrementing | Replays and corrections must be safe to apply more than once | Not append/increment (a re-applied correction would double-count) | Requires the TSDB to support upsert by (key,minute) |

> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
> **Side output** — a secondary result stream for events that don't fit the main window; here it carries too-late events to a correction step instead of dropping them.
> **Upsert** — insert-or-overwrite by key; here it makes a rollup correction idempotent so re-applying it can't double-count.

**Late-event correction flow**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FLINK[Flink keyed aggregator]
    FLINK -->|"1 within grace update window"| STATE[(RocksDB keyed state)]
    FLINK -->|"2 past grace side output"| SIDE[[Late events side stream]]
    subgraph Correct["Correction loop"]
        SIDE -->|"3 read late event"| CORR[Correction worker]
        CORR -->|"4 recompute minute"| TSDB[(Time series store)]
    end
    CORR -.->|"5 notify seal update"| HUB[Live push hub]
    classDef svc    fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue  fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db     fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class FLINK,CORR,HUB svc
    class SIDE queue
    class STATE,TSDB db
```
↳ reuses existing: Flink aggregator, RocksDB state, TSDB, Live push hub (from §6.2, §6.3)

**NUMBERED STEP NARRATION**
1. **Within grace, update the window.** If a late event arrives before allowed-lateness expires, the still-held window absorbs it and re-emits the corrected rollup. *State after:* minute corrected in-stream. *What could go wrong:* nothing — this is the happy path for normal lateness.
2. **Past grace, route to side output.** Events later than the grace period can't reuse closed state, so they go to a side stream. *State after:* late event captured, not dropped. *What could go wrong:* flood of very-late events (mass reconnect after an outage) → the side stream is bounded; if it overflows it spills, never blocks the main pipeline.
3. **Correction worker reads the late event.** A separate, lower-priority worker handles corrections off the hot path. *State after:* correction in progress. *What could go wrong:* worker down → side stream buffers; corrections are eventual, the live number is unaffected.
4. **Recompute the minute and upsert.** The worker re-derives that (key, minute) value and overwrites it in the TSDB. *State after:* historical minute corrected. *What could go wrong:* applied twice → upsert is idempotent, value is the same.
5. **Notify the hub of the corrected minute.** If a viewer is looking at that range, they see it tick to the corrected value. *State after:* boards consistent. *What could go wrong:* viewer already navigated away → no-op.

> 💬 "The rule is: never drop a late order, because 'late orders' is literally the metric ops watches. Within a few minutes I correct in-stream; beyond that I route to a side path that overwrites the right minute idempotently — so corrections converge and never double-count."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Late events are first-class here because 'late orders' is the headline metric — dropping them would corrupt the exact number ops cares about. I keep each minute's window open for a short grace period to absorb normal stragglers in-stream. Anything later than that gets routed to a side output and corrected by a separate worker that overwrites that minute idempotently in the store — so even a flood of very-late events after a network outage converges to the right answer without double-counting and without stalling the live pipeline. The trade-off is that very-late corrections are eventual, not instant, which is exactly the right call for an operational dashboard."

## 7. Data Model & Storage

**PART A — ENTITY TABLE**

| Entity | Key fields (PK / shard key annotated) | Chosen store | Shard / partition key | Consistency guarantee |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| OrderEvent (in transit) | **eventId (PK, dedup key)**, orderId, type, restaurantId, eventTime, promisedTime | Kafka log (3d) + S3 | **region+restaurantId** (partition) | Ordered per key, at-least-once delivery |
| Window state (in-flight) | **(restaurantId, windowStart) (PK)**, runningCounts, dedupSet | RocksDB (Flink keyed state) | **restaurantId** | Exactly-once via checkpoint |
| Rollup / MetricPoint | **(scope, id, metric, minute) (PK)**, value | Time-series store | **(scope, id)** | Read-committed, idempotent upsert |
| Restaurant / Region | **restaurantId (PK)**, regionId, promisedPrepSla | Relational (reference) + cache | restaurantId | Strong (rarely changes) |

**PART B — STORAGE DECISION CARDS**

**🗄️ Kafka (event log) — used for: OrderEvent firehose, replay, archive**
1. **What it is:** a durable append-only log — a tape recorder for events — that buffers a firehose and lets many consumers read independently at their own pace.
2. **Why we picked it:** we need to absorb ~208K events/s without loss, keep events ordered per restaurant for correct windowing, and *replay* days of history to recover from a bad deploy. Only a partitioned, retained log gives all three.
3. **What we considered instead:**
   - **SQS / managed queue** — no replay, no per-key ordering; a window can't trust event order, and we can't reprocess.
   - **Direct DB writes** — no database absorbs 200K writes/s cheaply, and we'd lose the buffer that protects against a slow aggregator.
   - **Kinesis** — works (same model) but more vendor lock-in; Kafka is the portable standard.
4. **Trade-off:** operational cost of running Kafka at this throughput and sizing partitions for peak.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "I put a durable log between the firehose and the aggregator. It buffers three days, keeps each restaurant's events ordered so my counts are right, and lets me replay if a deploy goes bad. A plain queue can't replay or keep order, so it was out."

**🗄️ Time-series store (TSDB) — used for: Rollups / MetricPoints (the served truth)**
1. **What it is:** a database tuned for timestamped metrics, with heavy compression and fast time-range scans — built for "value of this metric over the last N minutes."
2. **Why we picked it:** every read is exactly that shape, at ~60K/s. A TSDB compresses time-series ~10x (delta encoding), does downsampling and retention natively, and returns range scans in milliseconds.
3. **What we considered instead:**
   - **Postgres** — range-scanning billions of points is slow, no native downsampling/retention; we'd hand-build all of it.
   - **Cassandra** — would work as a wide-column time-series, but we'd reinvent the rollup/downsample/retention machinery a TSDB gives for free.
   - **Redis only** — great for the hottest current minute, but can't hold 90 days affordably; we use it as a thin front cache, not the store.
4. **Trade-off:** less flexible than SQL for ad-hoc joins — fine, because the access pattern is fixed.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Rollups go in a time-series store because every query is 'this metric over the last few minutes' — that's exactly what a TSDB is built for, and it compresses about ten-to-one. I considered Postgres but range scans over billions of points are slow, and I'd have to build downsampling and retention myself."

**🗄️ RocksDB (Flink keyed state) — used for: in-flight Window state**
1. **What it is:** an embedded on-disk key-value store living inside each stream task, holding each restaurant's running window tallies and dedup set.
2. **Why we picked it:** at 1M restaurants × several open windows, state won't fit in memory; RocksDB spills to local disk and is snapshotted by Flink's checkpoints, which is what makes counting exactly-once.
3. **What we considered instead:**
   - **In-memory state** — OOMs at this key count; no durability across restarts.
   - **External DB per update** — a network round-trip per event at 200K/s is impossible latency- and cost-wise.
4. **Trade-off:** disk I/O and checkpoint size to manage; large state slows recovery.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "The aggregator's running tallies live in on-disk state right next to the compute, because a million keys won't fit in memory and hitting an external database per event would be far too slow. Flink snapshots that state for exactly-once."

**PART C — Per-operation consistency summary**

| Operation | Store | Consistency level | Why that level is right here |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ingest event append | Kafka | At-least-once + ordered per key | Never lose an event; dedup handles the "at-least" part downstream |
| Window count → seal | RocksDB + checkpoint | Exactly-once | A double-counted order corrupts the metric; checkpoints guarantee it |
| Write sealed rollup | TSDB | Idempotent upsert (read-committed) | Replays/corrections must be safe to apply more than once |
| Dashboard read | TSDB | Eventual (bounded ~5s) | Operational board tolerates a few seconds of lag; that's the whole premise |
| Read-your-own-writes | TSDB + watermark | Honest staleness | The response's watermark tells the UI exactly which minutes are sealed vs still filling |

**PART D — DATA LIFECYCLE (largest table = Rollups)**

The rollup table is the largest *served* table at ~7 TB for 90 days (from §3). Retention is **90 days hot** in the TSDB, tied to that ~7 TB / ~$80K-of-the-$300K monthly figure — without a cap it grows unbounded. Reclamation is by **time-partitioned tables with a partition DROP** (instant, O(1)) rather than row-by-row DELETE (which churns the index and never cleanly reclaims space). Beyond 90 days, **downsample** 1-minute points to 1-hour and tier to S3 for cheap long-term history. The raw event log (~1.2 TB/day) keeps only **3 days hot** in Kafka, then archives to S3 for replay — it's never the serving store.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The serving store is a time-series database because the read pattern is fixed — metric over a time range — and it compresses around ten-to-one, so 90 days of rollups is only about seven terabytes. Behind it, the in-flight aggregation state lives in on-disk RocksDB next to the compute, snapshotted for exactly-once. Kafka is the durable buffer and replay source, three days hot then S3. I considered Postgres for the rollups but rejected it — range scans over billions of points are slow and I'd have to build downsampling and retention myself. Old data is reclaimed by dropping whole time partitions, never row deletes, and downsampled before it tiers to cold storage."

## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great

> 🆘 **If you get stuck:** go back to the two invariants — never lose an event, never count it twice — and reason forward from "where could a count drift?"

### How do we count exactly-once at 200K events/s?

**#### Bad: Naive consumer increments a counter**

🗣️ **What this is in plain words:** a worker reads each event off the stream and does "orders for this minute +1" directly in the database.

**Approach:** consumer reads from Kafka, on each event runs an UPDATE rollups SET orders = orders + 1. **Why people try this:** it's the obvious, simplest thing.

⚠️ **What breaks:** Kafka is *at-least-once* — on any consumer restart or rebalance, the last batch is re-read, so those events increment twice. At 200K/s a single rebalance double-counts tens of thousands of orders. Worse, the increment isn't tied to the read offset, so a crash between "increment" and "commit offset" silently double-counts on replay. The number drifts up all day and ops stops trusting it.

🔁 **What forces the upgrade:** the at-least-once + non-atomic-offset combo means any restart corrupts counts — unacceptable for NFR2.

**#### Good: Dedup by event id + offset commit**

↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** Bad incremented blindly with no idempotency, so replays double-counted. Good adds a dedup key so a re-read event is recognized and dropped.

🗣️ **What this is in plain words:** stamp every event with a unique id, remember which ids you've already counted, and ignore any you've seen before.

**Approach:** keep a dedup set (the `eventId`s seen for each open window) in state. On each event: if its id is already in the set, drop it; else count it and add the id. Commit Kafka offsets after the count. **Why it's better:** replays are now safe within the dedup window.

⚠️ **What breaks:** the dedup set grows unbounded unless TTL'd, and it must survive a crash — if it's only in memory, a crash loses it and we're back to double-counting. The offset commit and the count still aren't *atomic*: a crash between them re-reads and the dedup set must already contain those ids, but if the set wasn't checkpointed with the offsets, they disagree.

🔁 **What forces the upgrade:** dedup state and read offsets must be snapshotted *together*, atomically, or a crash desyncs them.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[Event log Kafka]
    K -->|"1 read event"| C[Consumer]
    C -->|"2 seen id check"| D[(Dedup set in state)]
    C -->|"3 count if new"| RB[Rollup builder]
    C -.->|"4 commit offset"| K
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class K queue
    class C,RB svc
    class D db
```

**#### Great: Checkpointed exactly-once with transactional sink**

↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** Good kept dedup state and offsets separately, so a crash could desync them. Great snapshots *all* state plus offsets in one atomic checkpoint, and commits to the sink transactionally.

🗣️ **What this is in plain words:** every so often, freeze the whole picture — counts, dedup memory, and exactly where we were in the stream — into one snapshot; on a crash, rewind to that frozen picture and replay, so nothing is lost and nothing is counted twice.

**Approach:** Flink's checkpoint barrier flows through the job; each operator snapshots its keyed state (counts + dedup set) and its Kafka read offset together to S3 as one consistent checkpoint. The TSDB write uses a **two-phase commit sink** — rollups are *pre-committed* on checkpoint and *finalized* only when the checkpoint completes, so a replay re-pre-commits the same (key, minute) value idempotently and never duplicates. On crash, the job restores the last checkpoint and resumes from the saved offsets.

🔢 **Decision-forcing math:** at 200K/s with a 30s checkpoint interval, a crash replays at most ~6M events, all idempotent — recovery is bounded and correct. The dedup set per window is tiny (event ids for ~minutes of one restaurant's orders), so state stays small even at 1M keys.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[Event log Kafka]
    K -->|"1 read with offset"| F[Flink operator]
    F -->|"2 update count and dedup"| S[(Keyed state)]
    F -.->|"3 barrier snapshot state plus offset"| CP[(Checkpoint S3)]
    F -->|"4 pre commit rollup"| TS[(Time series store)]
    CP -.->|"5 on complete finalize"| TS
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class K queue
    class F svc
    class S,CP,TS db
```

✅ **Failure matrix**

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Normal | Event counted once, checkpointed, finalized |
| Consumer crash before checkpoint | Restore last checkpoint, replay from saved offset, dedup set drops re-reads → exactly-once |
| Crash between pre-commit and finalize | On restart, checkpoint incomplete → pre-commit discarded and redone idempotently → no duplicate |
| Duplicate event from producer | Same eventId already in dedup set → dropped |
| Sink (TSDB) write fails | Retried idempotently on same (key, minute) → overwrite, never duplicate |
| Late event after seal | Side output → correction worker upserts → converges |

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Exactly-once is the spine of this system. The naive version increments a counter per event, but Kafka redelivers on any restart, so counts drift up and ops loses trust. Dedup by event id helps but isn't enough, because the dedup memory and the read position can disagree after a crash. The real fix is Flink's checkpointing: it freezes all the counts, the dedup memory, and the exact stream positions into one atomic snapshot, and it writes rollups in a two-phase commit so a replay re-writes the same value instead of adding to it. On a crash it rewinds to the snapshot and replays — bounded, and provably no double-counts."

> 🧠 **If they ask "why not just make the DB write idempotent and skip Flink's machinery?":** "Idempotent writes alone don't fix *windowed counts* — an increment isn't idempotent, and you'd have to materialize the full set membership somewhere. Flink gives me exactly-once over stateful aggregation for free; rebuilding it correctly is months of work."

---

### How do we handle a hot region or celebrity restaurant shard?

**#### Bad: One task per region key**

🗣️ **Plain words:** send all of a region's events to one worker and let it count.

**Approach:** key by region, one stateful task owns each region. **Why people try this:** simplest mental model — region in, count out.

⚠️ **What breaks:** the hottest region peaks at ~17K/s today, but a Super Bowl city or a stadium can spike a single key past the ~50K/s a single stateful task can sustain. That one task becomes a hot shard — its lag grows, its checkpoints slow, and because Kafka keeps the partition ordered, everything behind it stalls. One celebrity restaurant (a viral ghost kitchen) does the same at the restaurant level.

🔁 **What forces the upgrade:** any single key above ~50K/s (the flip threshold) overwhelms its task — and you can't add tasks for *one* key by keying alone.

**#### Good: Pre-aggregate (local combine) before the shuffle**

↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** Bad shuffled every raw event to one keyed task, concentrating load. Good shrinks the data *before* the shuffle so the hot key receives partial sums, not raw events.

🗣️ **Plain words:** before sending events to the per-region counter, have each upstream worker tally up its own little batch and send only the subtotal.

**Approach:** a local pre-aggregation operator (a combiner) maintains partial counts per key per short interval, then emits one partial per interval to the keyed step. The hot region's keyed task now receives N partials per second instead of N×events. **Why it's better:** combiner runs in parallel across all source partitions; the hot key only ever sees pre-summed data.

⚠️ **What breaks:** a single restaurant whose *own* event rate exceeds one combiner slot's capacity still concentrates — pre-aggregation helps regions but a true single-key firehose needs more.

🔁 **What forces the upgrade:** a single restaurant key above the per-slot ceiling.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[Event log Kafka]
    K -->|"1 raw events"| PA[Local combiners]
    PA -->|"2 partial sums"| AGG[Region keyed aggregator]
    AGG -->|"3 final rollup"| TS[(Time series store)]
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class K queue
    class PA,AGG svc
    class TS db
```

**#### Great: Key-splitting (salted sub-keys) + two-stage rollup**

↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** Good pre-aggregated but still routed one key to one final task. Great splits a hot key into N sub-keys so even a single restaurant's load fans across N tasks, then re-combines.

🗣️ **Plain words:** for a too-hot restaurant, pretend it's ten restaurants (restaurant#0..restaurant#9), count each in parallel, then add the ten back together.

**Approach:** detect hot keys (event rate over threshold), append a random salt 0..N to the key so its events spread across N parallel tasks (stage 1), each producing a partial; a second stage sums the N partials back into the true key (stage 2). Salt N is chosen so each sub-key stays under ~50K/s.

🔢 **Decision-forcing math:** a 100K/s celebrity restaurant ÷ N=4 sub-keys = 25K/s each, comfortably under the ~50K/s task ceiling; stage-2 re-combine handles only 4 partials/interval per key — trivial.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[Event log Kafka]
    K -->|"1 hot key detected"| SALT[Salt sub keys]
    SALT -->|"2 parallel partials"| S1[Stage one aggregators]
    S1 -->|"3 partial per sub key"| S2[Stage two recombine]
    S2 -->|"4 true key rollup"| TS[(Time series store)]
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class K queue
    class SALT,S1,S2 svc
    class TS db
```

✅ **Failure matrix**

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Normal key | No salt; single task; cheap |
| Hot region | Combiners pre-aggregate → keyed task sees partials |
| Celebrity restaurant | Salted into N sub-keys → fans across N tasks → recombined |
| Salt too small (still hot) | Auto-scaler bumps N; sub-keys re-spread on next window |
| Stage-2 recombine lag | Bounded — only N partials per key per interval |

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Hot shards are the classic streaming trap. If I just key by region, a stadium or a Super Bowl city sends one key past what a single task can handle, and because the partition is ordered, that one slow task stalls everything behind it. First fix: pre-aggregate locally before the shuffle, so the hot key receives subtotals instead of raw events. The real fix for a single viral restaurant is key-splitting — I salt the key into, say, four sub-keys so its load fans across four tasks, then a tiny second stage adds the four back together. That keeps every sub-key under the fifty-thousand-a-second ceiling."

> 🧠 **If they ask "how do you detect a hot key in time?":** "I track per-key event rate in the combiner and flip on salting when it crosses a threshold; the next window's events for that key get salted. It's reactive but the window granularity is a minute, so the lag to react is seconds."

---

### How do we keep the dashboard fresh without overloading anything (back-pressure)?

**#### Good: Bounded buffers + consumer lag as the signal**

🗣️ **Plain words:** if the aggregator can't keep up, let events pile safely in the log and watch how far behind we are.

**Approach:** Kafka *is* the buffer; the signal is **consumer lag** (how many events sit unread). Flink's network buffers are bounded, so back-pressure propagates upstream naturally — a slow sink slows the source's read rate, and unread events safely accumulate in Kafka (which is sized for 3 days). **Why it's better than dropping:** events are never lost, just delayed.

⚠️ **What breaks:** if lag grows unbounded (a sustained traffic surge beyond capacity), freshness silently degrades past 5s and nobody knows until ops complains.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[Event log Kafka]
    K -->|"1 read"| F[Flink]
    F -->|"2 bounded buffers"| TS[(Time series store)]
    F -.->|"3 lag metric"| MON[Lag monitor]
    MON -.->|"4 burn rate alert"| OC[On call]
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class K queue
    class F,MON svc
    class TS db
```

**#### Great: Lag-triggered autoscale + load-shed order**

↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** Good let lag grow until a human noticed. Great reacts automatically and defines what to sacrifice first.

🗣️ **Plain words:** when we fall behind, automatically add workers; if we still can't keep up, drop the least-important work first — never the order counts.

**Approach:** consumer lag over a threshold for >1 min triggers autoscaling of Flink task slots (and partition consumers). If lag still climbs past a hard ceiling, **shed in order:** first drop courier location pings (cosmetic), then prep heartbeats, *always* preserving order lifecycle events (the money/correctness metrics). The shed decision is at the gateway by event `type`.

🔢 **Decision-forcing math:** location pings are ~70% of the ~200K/s firehose; shedding them alone drops load to ~60K/s, instantly recovering headroom while every order/late metric stays exact.

✅ **Failure matrix**

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Brief spike | Bounded buffers absorb; lag self-heals |
| Sustained surge | Lag alert → autoscale task slots |
| Beyond max capacity | Shed location pings first, then heartbeats; lifecycle events always kept |
| Backlog after recovery | Replay from Kafka; freshness catches up; counts stay exact |

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Back-pressure is graceful by design because Kafka is the shock absorber — if the aggregator slows, events pile up safely in the log instead of being lost, and consumer lag is my early-warning signal. When lag climbs, I autoscale the aggregator. If I'm still underwater, I shed in a deliberate order: location pings go first because they're cosmetic, heartbeats next, and the order-lifecycle events — the ones that drive orders and late counts — are never dropped. Since pings are most of the firehose, shedding them alone buys back huge headroom while every metric ops cares about stays exact."

> 🧠 **If they ask "why not drop oldest events to catch up?":** "Dropping by age loses real orders and corrupts counts. Dropping by *type* sheds only cosmetic load and protects correctness — the operational decision is to be late on pings, never wrong on orders."

---

## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost

**9A — Availability targets**

- **Ingest path: four nines (99.99%)** — under ~52 minutes/year. Ingest loss is *permanent* (a blind minute can't be recovered), so it's the highest bar. Achieved with a multi-AZ Kafka cluster — multiple data centres in one region so if one building loses power the others keep accepting writes — replication factor 3, and producer-side retries/buffering.
- **Read path: three nines (99.9%)** — under ~8.7 hours/year. A read outage is recoverable (the data's still there), so a slightly lower bar is fine. Achieved with stateless query replicas behind a load balancer and a TSDB with replicas.

**9B — Per-dependency failure table**

| Component | What breaks if it's down | How it degrades — what the USER SEES | Recovery |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ingest gateway | New events can't enter | Producers retry with backoff; brief gap then catches up | Stateless — replace pods, autoscale |
| Kafka | No buffer; ingest stalls | Producers buffer locally up to a limit; if exceeded, oldest cosmetic events shed | Multi-AZ failover; replay on recovery |
| Flink aggregator | No new rollups | Dashboard freezes at last sealed minute, marked "stale as of HH:MM" | Restore from checkpoint, replay from Kafka offset |
| Time-series store | Reads fail | Query service serves last-good from a thin Redis front cache; "data may be delayed" banner | Replica promotion; multi-AZ |
| Live push hub | No push updates | Dashboard silently falls back to 5s polling — barely noticeable | Stateless reconnect to another hub node |
| Correction worker | Late events not corrected | Live numbers fine; historical minutes corrected once it recovers | Side stream buffers; drains on recovery |

**9C — RPO / RTO per data class**

> **RPO** (Recovery Point Objective) = how much data we'd lose if it crashed right now. RPO≈0 = we lose nothing. **RTO** (Recovery Time Objective) = how long until service is back.

| Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| Raw events (Kafka) | ≈0 | ~minutes | RF=3 multi-AZ; producer acks=all before ack |
| Aggregation state | last checkpoint (~30s) | ~minutes | Checkpoint to S3; replay re-derives the gap exactly-once |
| Rollups (TSDB) | rebuildable | ~minutes | Replay from Kafka if corrupted; not a system of record |

The key insight: rollups are **rebuildable** — if the TSDB is lost, I replay 3 days of Kafka through the pipeline and regenerate them. The only truly precious data is the raw event log, which is why it gets RPO≈0.

**9D — Cost breakdown**

Top 3 drivers (from §3, ~$300K/mo total):
1. **Kafka/MSK ~$135K/mo** — sustaining the 200K/s firehose with 3 days retention × RF=3 means a lot of brokers and disk. *This is the biggest line item* — the durable buffer, not the database.
2. **Flink stream compute ~$72K/mo** — an always-on cluster sized for *peak* parallelism (it can't scale to zero) plus checkpoint overhead.
3. **TSDB serving ~$80K/mo** — memory + disk for 90 days of rollups at 60K reads/s.

**Biggest optimization:** shed/sample location pings (~70% of the firehose) — they barely move the operational metrics but dominate Kafka and Flink cost. Sampling pings to 1-in-4 could cut the two biggest line items by half, taking unit economics from ~$3.32 toward ~$1.70 per 1M events.

**Observability:** golden signals — **traffic** (events/s in, reads/s), **latency** (event-time-to-sealed freshness p95), **errors** (rejected events, failed sinks), **saturation** (consumer lag, checkpoint duration, RocksDB size). **Burn-rate alert** when freshness p95 > 5s for 2 min, or consumer lag > 1 min of events. **Trace-id** propagates gateway → Kafka header → Flink → sink so one order's journey is traceable across the async hop.

**Rate-limiting:** the read API uses a **token bucket** per ops tenant (state in Redis — itself a global coordinator, sized accordingly); on reject, HTTP **429 + Retry-After**.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "When the aggregator dies, the dashboard doesn't go blank — it freezes on the last sealed minute with an honest 'stale as of' marker, and recovers by replaying from the durable log, so no counts are lost. When the store has trouble, reads fall back to a thin cache with a 'data may be delayed' banner. The only data I treat as precious is the raw event log, because everything else — all the rollups — I can rebuild by replaying it. On cost, the surprise is that the durable buffer and the always-on stream cluster cost more than the database, and the single biggest lever is sampling location pings, which are most of the firehose but barely move the operational numbers."

---

## 10. Trade-off Ledger

**🔀 Decision: Streaming aggregation (Flink) vs Lambda architecture (speed + batch layers)**
1. **What we chose & why:** a single streaming pipeline with exactly-once and late-event correction — one codebase, one source of truth.
2. **What we gave up:** the safety net of a separate batch layer that recomputes everything nightly to guarantee correctness.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** like trusting one careful accountant who fixes mistakes as they go, instead of one fast clerk plus a nightly auditor.
4. **When this reverses:** if events can be *hours* late (Fork C) or the numbers become billing-grade, I'd add a batch correction layer (Lambda/Kappa hybrid) that reprocesses from the S3 archive nightly.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "I went with one streaming pipeline because Flink's exactly-once plus a side-output correction path covers the lateness I expect. If lateness were unbounded or these fed billing, I'd add a nightly batch recompute."

**🔀 Decision: Approximately-correct live + eventual corrections vs strictly sealed-only**
1. **What we chose & why:** show the current minute as "still filling" and correct late arrivals after the fact, for freshness.
2. **What we gave up:** the live current-minute number can wobble slightly before it settles.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** like a live sports score that ticks up as plays are confirmed, rather than waiting for the official final.
4. **When this reverses:** if ops needs a number they can quote externally with zero wobble, I'd only ever show sealed windows and accept ~1 min more lag.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "I let the current minute show as provisional with a watermark, because for operations fresh-and-close beats late-and-perfect."

**🔀 Decision: TSDB serving store vs general-purpose DB**
1. **What we chose & why:** a time-series store because every read is metric-over-time and it compresses ~10x.
2. **What we gave up:** flexible ad-hoc SQL joins across dimensions.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** a specialized tool that's superb at one shape of question, not a Swiss-army knife.
4. **When this reverses:** if product wants rich ad-hoc slicing (by item, by courier, arbitrary joins), I'd add an OLAP store fed by the same stream for exploratory queries, keeping the TSDB for the live board.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Rollups go in a time-series store because the question is always 'this metric over time.' If we need ad-hoc slicing later, I'd fan the stream into an OLAP store too."

**CAP/PACELC:** on the ingest path we favor **AP** — under a partition we keep accepting events into the log (availability) and reconcile counts via dedup/replay rather than refusing writes; we never want to *drop* an order event. Under normal operation (PACELC's "else") we favor **latency** — we serve the freshest available rollup with a watermark rather than blocking for global consistency.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The decisions I'm least sure of: one streaming pipeline versus a batch safety net — I chose streaming because Flink's exactly-once plus a correction path covers my expected lateness, and I'd only add a batch layer if events could be hours late or fed billing. I show the live minute as provisional with a watermark for freshness, which I'd tighten to sealed-only if numbers had to be quoted externally. And I picked a time-series store over a general DB for the fixed read shape, adding OLAP later only if ad-hoc slicing matters. On the core path I'm AP and latency-favoring — I'd rather accept every event and reconcile than refuse a write."

---

## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers

**❓ "Two events for the same order arrive twice — does the count go wrong?"**
**Mechanism:** every event carries a producer-generated `eventId`; the aggregator holds a per-window dedup set in checkpointed state and drops any id it's already counted. Flink's checkpoint snapshots that set together with the Kafka read offset, so even a crash-and-replay can't double-count.
🗣️ **In plain words:** each event has a fingerprint; we remember the fingerprints we've counted and ignore repeats, and our "save point" remembers both the counts and where we were in the stream.
💬 **One-liner:** "Dedup by event id inside exactly-once checkpointing — a replay re-reads but never re-counts."

**❓ "An event shows up 10 minutes late — what happens to it?"**
**Mechanism:** within the allowed-lateness grace period the still-held window absorbs it in-stream and re-emits; past the grace it's routed to a side output and a correction worker upserts (overwrites) that minute's value in the TSDB idempotently.
🗣️ **In plain words:** if it's a little late we fix the minute on the fly; if it's very late we patch that historical minute afterward — and we never just drop it, because late orders is the metric ops watches.
💬 **One-liner:** "Within grace we correct in-stream; beyond it a side path overwrites the right minute, never an increment."

**❓ "What if the Flink job crashes mid-window?"**
**Mechanism:** it restores from the last checkpoint (state + offsets, ~30s old) and replays from those offsets; the two-phase-commit sink discards any un-finalized rollups and redoes them idempotently.
🗣️ **In plain words:** it rewinds to its last save point and replays the few seconds it lost, with no double-counting.
💬 **One-liner:** "Restore checkpoint, replay from saved offset — bounded recovery, exactly-once preserved."

**❓ "Kafka goes down — do we lose events?"**
**Mechanism:** Kafka is multi-AZ with replication factor 3 and producers use acks=all, so a write is acknowledged only after replicas confirm; an AZ loss fails over to surviving replicas with RPO≈0.
🗣️ **In plain words:** each event is copied to three machines in different buildings before we say "got it," so one building dying loses nothing.
💬 **One-liner:** "RF=3 multi-AZ, acks=all — RPO is effectively zero on the precious data."

**❓ "How do you handle a Super Bowl spike or a viral restaurant (hot shard)?"**
**Mechanism:** local combiners pre-aggregate before the keyed shuffle so a hot key sees subtotals, not raw events; for a single key past ~50K/s we salt it into N sub-keys that fan across N tasks and recombine in a second stage.
🗣️ **In plain words:** we tally things up early so the busy counter gets summaries not raw data, and for one insanely busy restaurant we split it into several and add them back up.
💬 **One-liner:** "Pre-aggregate before the shuffle; salt hot keys into sub-keys, recombine — keeps every task under the 50K ceiling."

**❓ "The pipeline can't keep up during peak — then what?"**
**Mechanism:** Kafka buffers (3 days), consumer lag is the signal; sustained lag autoscales task slots, and if still behind we shed by event type — location pings first, lifecycle events never.
🗣️ **In plain words:** events wait safely in the log, we add workers, and if we're still drowning we stop processing the cosmetic stuff (location dots) but never the order counts.
💬 **One-liner:** "Bounded buffers + lag-triggered autoscale; shed pings first, protect the order metrics."

**❓ "Can the live number be wrong?"**
**Mechanism:** the current (unsealed) minute is provisional and labeled with a watermark; sealed minutes are exactly-once correct, and late corrections converge sealed values to truth.
🗣️ **In plain words:** the minute happening right now is a live estimate clearly marked as such; finished minutes are exact.
💬 **One-liner:** "Sealed minutes are exact; the current one is provisional and labeled — fresh beats falsely-precise."

**❓ "How do you stop a Chicago manager from seeing New York's numbers?"**
**Mechanism:** caller identity comes from a JWT; object-level authz checks the requested region against the user's assigned regions before any read.
🗣️ **In plain words:** we know who's asking from their login token and only let them see the regions they're responsible for.
💬 **One-liner:** "JWT identity plus object-level authz on region — not just logged-in, but allowed to see THIS region."

**❓ "Could someone forge inflated numbers by faking events?"**
**Mechanism:** ingest is mTLS service-to-service only (the order service, not clients), and the server derives region and lateness itself; clients never submit aggregates, only raw lifecycle events from a trusted producer.
🗣️ **In plain words:** only our own order service can push events, over a mutually-authenticated channel, and it can't hand us pre-cooked totals.
💬 **One-liner:** "mTLS-only ingest from a trusted producer; the server computes the metrics, nobody submits them."

**❓ "What's the most expensive part, and how would you cut it?"**
**Mechanism:** the durable buffer (Kafka, ~$135K/mo) and the always-on stream cluster (~$72K/mo) dominate, not storage; location pings are ~70% of the firehose, so sampling them 1-in-4 roughly halves both.
🗣️ **In plain words:** the firehose buffer and the 24/7 processor cost the most, and most of the firehose is cosmetic location dots we can sample down.
💬 **One-liner:** "Buffer + stream compute dominate; sampling location pings — most of the volume — is the biggest lever."

**❓ "If the time-series store is wiped, how bad is it?"**
**Mechanism:** rollups are rebuildable — replay 3 days of Kafka through the pipeline to regenerate them; only the raw event log is treated as precious.
🗣️ **In plain words:** the scoreboards aren't the source of truth, the event tape is — we just re-run the tape to rebuild them.
💬 **One-liner:** "Rollups are derived; replay the log to rebuild — the only precious data is the raw events."

**❓ "How would you add per-courier or per-menu-item metrics later?"**
**Mechanism:** add a new keying dimension to the same stream — a parallel keyed aggregation producing courier/item rollups into the same TSDB, no new ingest. Watch state growth as the keyspace multiplies.
🗣️ **In plain words:** we tap the same event stream and group it a different way; the events already carry courier and item.
💬 **One-liner:** "Same stream, new grouping key — additive, the events already carry the dimensions."

**❓ "How would you add anomaly alerting (e.g. 'region X suddenly 30% late')?"**
**Mechanism:** add a downstream stream operator that reads sealed rollups, compares against a baseline/threshold, and emits alert events — a new consumer of the existing rollup stream, no change to aggregation.
🗣️ **In plain words:** bolt on a watcher that reads the same scoreboards and shouts when a number jumps.
💬 **One-liner:** "A new consumer of the rollup stream does threshold detection — alerting is additive."

**❓ "Why event-time and not just when events arrive?"**
**Mechanism:** arrival-time buckets misfile any delayed or out-of-order event into the wrong minute; event-time (set at the source) plus watermarks bucket by when things actually happened, which is what "orders at 6:14pm" must mean.
🗣️ **In plain words:** we count by when something really happened, not when the news reached us, or the minutes would be wrong.
💬 **One-liner:** "Bucket by event-time, gate on watermarks — arrival-time would misfile every late event."

**❓ "What's your single point of serialization, and is it a bottleneck?"**
**Mechanism:** the per-key aggregator is the only serialization point (each key processed in order by one task); we relieve it with pre-aggregation and key-splitting so no single key exceeds the ~50K/s task ceiling.
🗣️ **In plain words:** each restaurant's events are counted in order by one worker; if one gets too busy we split it across several.
💬 **One-liner:** "Per-key task is the serialization point; pre-aggregate and salt to keep any key under the ceiling."

> 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "This is a live operations dashboard for a food-delivery platform — orders per minute, prep times, and late orders per restaurant and region, fresh within about five seconds. The design splits into two halves. The read half is simple: pre-computed rollups live in a time-series store that a stateless query service reads and a push hub streams to dashboards, so fifty thousand viewers see live updates without polling. The write half is the real system — a firehose of about two hundred thousand events a second lands in a durable Kafka log, and a Flink stream processor keeps per-restaurant minute windows in on-disk state. The hardest problem is counting exactly-once at that rate, and the mechanism that solves it is Flink's checkpointing plus a two-phase-commit sink, so a crash rewinds and replays with no double-counts, while late events are corrected through a side-output path that overwrites the right minute idempotently. The two halves connect through that durable log, which is also my replay and rebuild path. Every hard decision traces back to one principle: never lose an event and never count it twice — be late before you're wrong."

I've completed the full HLD session for "Design Real-Time Restaurant Order Metrics (Food Delivery)".

Summary of what was produced:

- **Output file:** `/Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/6cc56ef5-159e-4e63-b69c-f35c896eb28f_hld/_response.md` — 87,137 characters of pure markdown, all 11 sections (§1–§11) present with exact headings.
- **Capacity script:** `/Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/6cc56ef5-159e-4e63-b69c-f35c896eb28f_hld/capacity.py` — written and run with the venv python. Only rounded results surfaced.

Key design decisions and numbers:
- **Binding bottleneck:** ~208K events/s peak ingest (the firehose), ~3:1 write-heavy ratio — unusual, so budget goes to the ingest/aggregate path, not a read cache.
- **Flip threshold:** ~50K/s per key forces edge pre-aggregation and key-salting for hot regions/celebrity restaurants.
- **Pipeline:** events → Kafka (~32 partitions) → Flink keyed windowed aggregation (RocksDB state, exactly-once via checkpoints + 2PC sink) → time-series store, with a side-output correction loop for late/out-of-order events.
- **Cost:** ~$300K/mo (Kafka + Flink dominant, storage only ~7TB/$80K), ~$3.32 per 1M events; biggest lever is sampling location pings (~70% of firehose).

Verification passed:
- All 11 "## N." headings present in order; §1 opens with the scope-lock-first structure (4 vetoable forking assumptions → derived FRs → provisional NFRs); §2 is residual forks only, no re-asking.
- 10 mermaid blocks, all `flowchart TD`, scanned clean of breaking characters in labels.
- capacity.py runs; its numbers match what's surfaced in §3/§8/§9.

I did not write into `data/conversations/` since the prompt states the orchestrator assembles the JSON afterward.
